first_line_drugs.notes: TID x5-7 days, first-line for localised non-bullous impetigo

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based 6 doses over 3 days BID, take with food

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.3 mg/kg (max 10mg), preferred pre-hospital non-IV route

15-20 mg/kg IV loading, status epilepticus

18-54mg once daily AM, Concerta or Ritalin LA

1mg/day HS, titrate to 1-4mg/day, non-stimulant alpha-2 agonist

notes: Behaviour therapy first-line for age 4-5yr. Stimulants first-line for age 6yr+. Monitor growth, BP, HR.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.4 U/kg/day SC once daily at bedtime
0.05-0.1 U/kg/dose SC pre-meal, lispro or aspart

second_line_drugs.notes: Automated insulin delivery, preferred for all ages where available - ISPAD 2022

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg/day (max 5-10mg) once daily, long-acting CCB

1-3 mg/kg/day (max 50mg), volume-dependent hypertension

30-50 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses during acute phase, then 3-5 mg/kg/day once afebrile

For high-risk IVIG-resistant cases per Kobayashi/Egami scoring

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray/nostril (50mcg) once daily, age 4yr+, most effective single agent

second_line_drugs.notes: 5mg (age 2-5yr), 10mg (age 6yr+) once daily
4mg (2-5yr), 5mg (6-14yr) once daily. FDA 2020 Black Box Warning for neuropsychiatric effects

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg anterolateral thigh (max 0.5mg). Auto-injector: 0.15mg under 30kg / 0.3mg 30kg+. Repeat q5-15min if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus, hypotension or shock
1 mg/kg (max 50mg), adjunct only - never delays epinephrine
1-2 mg/kg (max 125mg), adjunct to reduce biphasic reaction risk

diagnosis_name: Fever and mild to moderate pain

5-10 mg/kg/dose q6-8h PO, max 40 mg/kg/day or 2.4g/day, age 6 months+

second_line_drugs.notes: q4h alternating schedule, evidence-based for sustained fever control

first_line_drugs.notes: Percutaneous; indicated for critical valvar AS in neonates who are ductal-dependent regardless of gradient

Open surgical repair for neonates/infants not amenable to balloon valvuloplasty

second_line_drugs.notes: Autograft pulmonary valve replacement of aortic valve with pulmonary homograft; gold standard for AVR in children
For older children when repair not feasible; requires lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin

guideline_changes: 2025 ACC/AHA/ISACHD guideline for congenital heart disease management released

first_line_drugs.notes: Small ASDs (<5mm) in children <2yr; many close spontaneously; serial echo monitoring q6-12mo

Open repair via sternotomy with CPB for large, sinus venosus, or primum ASDs not amenable to device

5-10 mcg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for RV failure symptoms if present pre-operatively

first_line_drugs.notes: Coarctectomy with extended end-to-end anastomosis; primary technique in neonates and infants

Preferred for discrete coarctation in children 4mo-5yr (<25kg) without arch hypoplasia

second_line_drugs.notes: Preferred in older children >25kg; endovascular stenting for native or recurrent coarctation
0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for residual hypertension post-repair, titrate to effect
0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day PO once daily (max 5mg); adjunct antihypertensive post-repair
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO once daily; beta-blocker for post-repair hypertension

guideline_changes: 2024 STS clinical practice guidelines published for neonatal/infant coarctation management

diagnosis_name: Congenital malformation of cardiac chambers and connections, unspecified

first_line_drugs.notes: Diagnostic and interventional; for defect characterization and percutaneous repair of simple lesions
Open-heart surgery tailored to specific defect anatomy; timing based on hemodynamic significance

5-10 mcg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for symptomatic heart failure support

first_line_drugs.notes: First-line for moderate-severe valvar PS (gradient >40mmHg); percutaneous via femoral vein; excellent outcomes

Surgical patch for subvalvar or supravalvar obstruction combined with valve repair

first_line_drugs.notes: Anastomosis of common pulmonary venous confluence to left atrium with ligation of anomalous connection; CPB required

first_line_drugs.notes: Jatene procedure; anatomic repair switching aorta and PA with coronary reimplantation; within first 2 weeks of life

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; ACE inhibitor for DCM with systolic dysfunction; first-line GDMT

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); first-line NSAID; taper over 2-4 weeks after symptom resolution

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/dose PO q8h (ages 1-17yr); FDA-approved for WHO Group 1 PAH; titrate based on response

diagnosis_name: Acute rheumatic fever without mention of heart involvement

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV rapid push (max 6mg); repeat at 0.2 mg/kg (max 12mg); first-line for SVT diagnosis/treatment
Loading 5 mg/kg IV over 30-60min; then 5-15 mcg/kg/min infusion; broad-spectrum antiarrhythmic

first_line_drugs.notes: Ice to face (infants) or Valsalva (older children); first-line non-pharmacologic intervention for SVT

first_line_drugs.notes: Asymptomatic anomalies (e.g., persistent LSVC) often require no treatment; serial echo monitoring

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply thin layer to affected area BID for 10-14 days; use lowest potency effective

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 2.5-5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 5-10 mg daily

2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose); for persistent itch

Apply to affected area 3-4x daily after diaper change for 7-14 days if candidal
Apply generously at each diaper change as barrier; use with frequent diaper changes

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; first-line for mild unspecified dermatitis
Apply liberally BID-TID and after bathing; fragrance-free ceramide-based preferred
6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 10 mg daily for pruritus

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply BID for 14 days; for moderate dermatitis on trunk/extremities

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 2.5-5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 5-10 mg daily

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to scalp, lather, leave 5 min then rinse; daily for 2 weeks then 1-2x/week

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area BID for 10-14 days; low potency for pediatric use
Apply liberally BID and after bathing; restore skin barrier
6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 10 mg daily for itch

Apply BID for face/intertriginous areas; steroid-sparing for children >=2 yr

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply thin layer to affected area daily; start low concentration and titrate; reduces resistance

second_line_drugs.notes: Children >8 yr & >45 kg: 50-100 mg PO daily-BID; <=45 kg: 2.2 mg/kg BID; 8-12 wk course

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected follicles TID for 7-10 days; for superficial bacterial folliculitis
Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for localized bacterial folliculitis

Apply BID for 2-4 weeks; for Malassezia (pityrosporum) folliculitis

first_line_drugs.notes: Move child to cool environment; remove excess clothing; allow skin to air dry

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected skin area TID-QID for 14 days; first-line for cutaneous candidiasis
Apply to affected area BID-TID for 7-14 days; broad-spectrum topical antifungal

Apply to affected nails daily for up to 48 weeks; for onychomycosis in older children
Apply to affected area daily for 2-4 weeks; alternative topical antifungal

notes: Keep affected skin dry and well-ventilated. For recurrent candidiasis, evaluate for predisposing factors (diabetes, immunodeficiency, prolonged antibiotic use).

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area daily-BID for 1-2 weeks; for tinea corporis/cruris/pedis
20-25 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g/day) for 6-8 weeks; for tinea capitis
Apply to affected area BID for 2-4 weeks; OTC topical for tinea corporis/pedis

second_line_drugs.notes: <20 kg: 62.5 mg/day; 20-40 kg: 125 mg/day; >40 kg: 250 mg/day PO for 4-6 weeks

guideline_changes: Rising T. indotineae antifungal resistance reported; CDC alert 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to body/scalp as wash, leave 5 min, rinse daily for 2 weeks; for pityriasis versicolor
Apply to affected area, leave 10 min, rinse daily for 1-2 weeks; OTC antifungal
Apply to affected area BID for 2-4 weeks; topical azole for localized disease

3-5 mg/kg/day PO for 5-7 days; for recurrent or widespread pityriasis versicolor
Apply as body wash, leave 5 min, rinse; use 2-3x/week; OTC maintenance option

notes: Pityriasis versicolor has high recurrence; discuss monthly prophylactic antifungal wash. Dyspigmentation may persist for months after successful treatment.

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO Q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); for pain and inflammation in EM minor

>=12 yr: 500 mg PO BID x 5 days; for HSV-associated recurrent EM; suppressive dosing

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO Q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); first-line NSAID for pain/inflammation
5-7 mg/kg/dose PO Q12h (max 500 mg/dose); alternative NSAID for older children
Bed rest with leg elevation; compressive bandaging; reduces inflammation and pain

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-23 mo: 2.5 mg PO daily; 2-5 yr: 5 mg daily; >=6 yr: 10 mg daily; for itch/urticaria
Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for localized erythematous rash
Apply liberally BID-TID; soothe and protect inflamed skin

2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose); for persistent pruritus

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to affected area TID-QID PRN for itch relief; lukewarm oatmeal baths may help
2-5 yr: 2.5-5 mg PO daily; >=6 yr: 5-10 mg daily; for symptomatic pruritus
Apply to itchy lesions BID for 1-2 weeks; for moderate pruritus

diagnosis_name: Diseases of pulp and periapical tissues

For fungal OE (otomycosis): 3-5 drops bid x14-21d after canal debridement
25-50mg/kg/day PO divided q6-8h x7-10d only if cellulitis extends beyond canal

first_line_drugs.notes: 80-90mg/kg/day PO divided bid-tid x10d (<2yr) or 7d (2-5yr) or 5d (>5yr), max 3g/day

5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/fever, max 40mg/kg/day

14mg/kg/day PO once daily or divided bid x10d, max 600mg/day; PCN-allergic (non-severe)
10mg/kg PO day 1 then 5mg/kg/day x4d for severe PCN allergy, max 250mg/day

first_line_drugs.notes: Comprehensive exam by low vision specialist; prescribe magnifiers, contrast aids, lighting
Maximize residual vision with best refraction; update q6-12mo in growing children
Certified O&M specialist; begin early; white cane training when ambulatory

JAWS, NVDA, or VoiceOver for school-age children; training by TVI specialist
Adaptive daily living skills; sensory integration; handwriting alternatives
Enroll by 6mo; developmental support, family training, per state EI program

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply 1cm ribbon to lower conjunctival sac qid x5-7d; all ages including neonates

notes: Bacterial conjunctivitis is most common in children; viral is self-limiting in 7-14d. Neonatal conjunctivitis (<30d) requires urgent evaluation to rule out gonococcal/chlamydial infection.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 firm downward strokes over lacrimal sac bid-tid; parents taught technique; age <12mo
Apply 1cm ribbon bid-tid for acute dacryocystitis/purulent discharge episodes

first_line_drugs.notes: Full cycloplegic refraction Rx; update q6-12mo; polycarbonate lenses for children
1 drop in each eye at bedtime daily for myopia control; age 4-12yr; monitor q6mo
For active children/sports; orthokeratology overnight lenses for myopia control option

guideline_changes: Low-dose atropine 0.01-0.05% now widely adopted for myopia control based on Phase 3 trial data

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop q1h around the clock x48h then taper to q2-4h based on response; age ≥1yr

1 drop bid-tid for ciliary spasm/pain relief; adjunct to antibiotic therapy

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop q1h x48h then taper; alternative 4th-gen fluoroquinolone monotherapy; ≥1yr
1 drop q1-2h for fungal keratitis; first-line for filamentous fungi
1 drop q1h for severe fungal keratitis; compounded; for Candida/resistant fungi
Apply 5x/day x14-21d for HSV keratitis; add oral acyclovir 20mg/kg q5h if stromal

notes: Bacterial keratitis is an ocular emergency; obtain corneal cultures before starting empiric therapy if central/severe. Contact lens wearers need Pseudomonas coverage with fluoroquinolone monotherapy.

first_line_drugs.notes: Correct full cycloplegic refraction; accommodative esotropia may fully resolve with glasses
Patch sound eye 2h/day for moderate amblyopia, 6h/day for severe; with near activities
1 drop in sound eye daily or weekend-only for moderate amblyopia; age <10yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Recession/resection of extraocular muscles; under GA; for deviations unresponsive to optical Rx
Inject into extraocular muscle under EMG guidance; for small-angle deviations or pre-surgical
Translucent filter on spectacle lens over sound eye; for moderate amblyopia; better compliance
Fresnel or ground-in prism for small residual deviations; for diplopia relief

notes: Treat amblyopia first before surgical correction of strabismus. Visual plasticity is greatest before age 7-9; early detection and treatment yield best outcomes.

first_line_drugs.notes: Full cycloplegic refraction, dilated fundus exam, visual field if cooperative; age-appropriate
Prescribe optimal refraction first; reassess 4-6wk; may resolve functional visual disturbance
For amblyopia: 2h/day moderate, 6h/day severe; combine with 1h near activities daily

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop in sound eye daily or weekend-only for amblyopia; equivalent to 2h patching
VR-based dichoptic therapy 1h/day x12wk for amblyopia; FDA-approved age 4-7yr
If unexplained visual loss, papilledema, optic atrophy, or neurologic signs present
Magnifiers, large-print materials, assistive tech if best-corrected VA remains impaired

notes: Visual disturbances in children require thorough evaluation to exclude retinal, optic nerve, or neurologic pathology. Functional/non-organic visual loss is a diagnosis of exclusion in the pediatric population.
guideline_changes: Digital therapeutic (Luminopia) FDA-approved 2021 as adjunct for amblyopia treatment in children 4-7yr

first_line_drugs.notes: Under direct otoscopic visualization; first-line for visible non-spherical objects
Body-temp water via syringe; for non-organic, non-battery objects; intact TM required
Frazier suction tip applied to FB surface; for smooth/round objects in cooperative child

second_line_drugs.notes: Blunt probe tip with dried glue applied to FB surface; for round objects; cooperative patient
Operating microscope + microinstruments; for failed ED attempts, deep FB, or uncooperative child
4 drops bid x5-7d post-removal if canal abrasion or infection present

notes: Button batteries require emergent removal due to risk of liquefactive necrosis; do NOT irrigate batteries. Limit removal attempts to 1-2 tries in ED; failed attempts increase complication risk and warrant ENT referral.

diagnosis_name: Foreign body in eye and adnexa

first_line_drugs.notes: Irrigate affected eye with 500-1000mL NS; evert lids; sweep fornices with moist cotton swab
1-2 drops for anesthesia before examination/removal; single use; do not prescribe for home
Moisten tip, roll gently over superficial conjunctival/corneal FB under slit lamp

second_line_drugs.notes: For embedded corneal FB; under topical anesthesia; by trained provider; ophthalmology referral
Apply 1cm ribbon qid x5-7d post-removal to prevent infection of corneal defect
1 drop qid x5-7d post-removal for corneal abrasion prophylaxis; age ≥1yr
1 drop bid-tid for ciliary spasm/photophobia after corneal FB removal

notes: Check visual acuity before any manipulation; fluorescein exam mandatory to assess corneal defect and rule out penetration. Young children may require sedation or OR removal under GA; ophthalmology consultation for embedded or metallic FBs.

first_line_drugs.notes: Within 2h for esophageal button battery or sharp object; within 24h for esophageal coin/food bolus
10mL PO q10min (max 6 doses) while awaiting endoscopy for esophageal button battery
10mL PO q10min alternating with honey while awaiting endoscopy for esophageal battery
AP chest/abdomen; confirm location and track progression; q12-24h if in stomach

second_line_drugs.notes: For small blunt objects past esophagus; stool monitoring; repeat XR if not passed in 2wk
GoLYTELY 25mL/kg/h via NGT for multiple magnets or drug packets beyond endoscope reach
0.02-0.03mg/kg IV (max 0.5mg) for esophageal food bolus; smooth muscle relaxant

notes: Button batteries in esophagus are a surgical emergency (remove <2h); magnets require urgent removal if >1 ingested due to bowel perforation risk. Most blunt objects past the esophagus pass spontaneously within 4-6 days.
guideline_changes: Honey/sucralfate pre-endoscopy for button battery ingestion now recommended (NASPGHAN 2023)

diagnosis_name: Chronic diseases of tonsils and adenoids

first_line_drugs.notes: For ≥7 episodes/1yr, ≥5/yr x2yr, or ≥3/yr x3yr of documented strep/tonsillitis; or OSA
50mg/kg/day PO divided bid-tid x10d for acute exacerbations of chronic tonsillitis
0.5mg/kg IV single dose intraop (max 10mg) for tonsillectomy; reduces PONV/pain

second_line_drugs.notes: 45mg/kg/day PO divided bid x10d for recurrent tonsillitis unresponsive to amoxicillin
20-30mg/kg/day PO divided tid x10d for chronic tonsillitis biofilm eradication; PCN allergy
5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN x7-10d post-tonsillectomy; safe per AAO-HNS 2019 update
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain post-tonsillectomy; max 75mg/kg/day

notes: Watchful waiting recommended if infection frequency is below Paradise criteria thresholds. Post-tonsillectomy: codeine is CONTRAINDICATED in children <12yr; ibuprofen and acetaminophen are mainstays of pain management.

first_line_drugs.notes: Pinch lower soft nose firmly x10-15min; lean forward; do not release to check
2 sprays in affected nostril; age ≥3yr; may apply on gauze and pack; max 3-5 days use
Apply thin layer to anterior septum bid x2-4wk to moisturize and prevent recurrence

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply stick to visible bleeding vessel on anterior septum x5-10sec; unilateral only per session
Soak gauze in 100mg/mL solution and pack nostril x30min; or 500mg in 5mL nebulized
Merocel sponge or ribbon gauze with antibiotic ointment; remove in 24-48h
2 sprays per nostril bid-tid for humidification and crust prevention; prophylactic

notes: Anterior epistaxis from Little's area (Kiesselbach plexus) accounts for >90% of pediatric nosebleeds. Recurrent or posterior epistaxis warrants workup for coagulopathy, hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, or nasal pathology.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray per nostril daily x6-12wk; age ≥2yr; 77% show improvement avoiding surgery
Isotonic saline 5-10mL per nostril bid via squeeze bottle or neti pot; all ages
Outpatient procedure under GA if medical therapy fails or severe obstruction/OSA/recurrent OME

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray per nostril daily x6-12wk; age ≥4yr; alternative intranasal steroid
4mg PO daily (2-5yr) or 5mg (6-14yr) as adjunct; FDA black box: neuropsychiatric risk
1.25mg PO daily (1-5yr), 2.5mg (6-11yr) if concurrent allergic rhinitis component
For mild symptoms without OSA; adenoid may involute with growth by age 7-10yr

notes: Trial of intranasal corticosteroids for 6-12 weeks is recommended before proceeding to adenoidectomy for uncomplicated hypertrophy. Surgery indicated for OSA, chronic OME with hearing loss, or chronic rhinosinusitis unresponsive to medical therapy.
guideline_changes: Intranasal mometasone now established as first-line medical trial before adenoidectomy (S2k 2023)

first_line_drugs.notes: Indicated for OSA/SDB with tonsillar hypertrophy; PSG recommended if <2yr, obese, or comorbid
1 spray per nostril daily trial x6wk for mild SDB before surgical decision; age ≥2yr
0.5mg/kg IV single dose intraop (max 10mg); reduces PONV and improves oral intake

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN x7-10d post-tonsillectomy; AAO-HNS confirms safety
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain post-tonsillectomy; max 75mg/kg/day
4mg (2-5yr) or 5mg (6-14yr) PO daily for persistent OSA post-surgery; FDA black box warning
If snoring without OSA/apnea and no impact on growth, behavior, or school performance

notes: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line for pediatric OSA with tonsillar hypertrophy; PSG should be obtained before surgery in high-risk groups. Codeine is contraindicated post-tonsillectomy in children <12yr due to fatal respiratory depression risk.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1:10,000) IV/IO q3-5min during cardiac arrest; max single dose 1 mg
100% FiO2 via bag-mask ventilation; titrate to SpO2 94-99% once ROSC achieved
20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus for volume resuscitation; repeat as needed up to 60 mL/kg

second_line_drugs.notes: Target 33-34°C for 72h in neonatal HIE; initiate within 6h of birth
2-5 mL/kg IV for hypoglycemia (glucose <60 mg/dL); recheck in 15 min
1 mEq/kg IV slow push if pH <7.0 and prolonged arrest; dilute 1:1 in neonates

notes: Prioritize airway management and ventilation; most pediatric arrests are respiratory in origin. Avoid hyperventilation; target normocapnia (ETCO2 35-40 mmHg) post-ROSC.
guideline_changes: 2025 AHA updated ventilation rates to 20-30 breaths/min for pediatric advanced airway

diagnosis_name: Burn and corrosion, unspecified body region

first_line_drugs.notes: 3 mL/kg/%TBSA over 24h; half in first 8h, rest over 16h; add D5 maintenance for <30 kg
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN pain; max 0.2 mg/kg/dose; titrate to comfort
Apply topically to burn wound BID after cleansing; avoid on face/near eyes
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; add TIG 250 units IM if incomplete primary series

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV for procedural sedation during debridement; 0.5 mg/kg for analgesia
Apply topically to deep burns BID; preferred for ear cartilage burns
0.5-1 g/kg IV if serum albumin <2 g/dL after 24h; adjunct to crystalloid

notes: Fluid resuscitation required for burns >10-15% TBSA in children. Monitor UOP target 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr; watch for fluid creep.
guideline_changes: Updated pediatric Parkland formula to 3 mL/kg/%TBSA with mandatory dextrose maintenance

diagnosis_name: Burns classified according to extent of body surface involved

first_line_drugs.notes: 3 mL/kg/%TBSA for first 24h; half in first 8h from time of burn; D5LR maintenance for <30 kg
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN; consider PCA for older children; titrate to pain score
Apply topically BID to partial/full thickness burns; reassess wound daily

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mcg/kg IV q1-2h PRN or 1-2 mcg/kg intranasal for procedural pain
Emergent surgical procedure for circumferential full-thickness burns with compartment syndrome
0.1 mg/kg PO BID for severe burns >20% TBSA to reduce catabolism; max 20 mg/day

notes: Transfer to burn center for >10% TBSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum, or electrical/chemical burns. Use Lund-Browder chart for accurate TBSA in children.
guideline_changes: 2024 Utah PTN guideline emphasizes early enteral nutrition within 6h for >20% TBSA burns

first_line_drugs.notes: Airway, breathing, circulation stabilization per ATLS; treat life-threatening injuries first
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h for pain; max 40 mg/kg/day; avoid if abdominal trauma suspected
20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus if signs of shock; repeat up to 60 mL/kg; assess after each bolus

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for moderate-severe pain; monitor respiratory status
2-5 mg IV/SC if coagulopathy detected; evaluate for abusive head trauma

notes: Mandatory CPS reporting required. Perform skeletal survey in all children <2 years with suspected abuse; use TEN-4-FACESp screening for bruising.
guideline_changes: ACS Best Practices for Trauma Center Recognition of Child Abuse effective Jan 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus; then aggressive fluid resuscitation targeting UOP 1-2 mL/kg/hr to prevent rhabdomyolysis
12-lead ECG on arrival; continuous monitoring for 24h minimum for all high-voltage injuries
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for pain; titrate to comfort; assess for compartment syndrome

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus then 150 mEq/L infusion to alkalinize urine if myoglobinuria present
0.5-1 g/kg IV if myoglobinuria persists despite fluids; maintain UOP >2 mL/kg/hr
0.1 mg/kg IV for seizures secondary to electrical injury; max 5 mg/dose

notes: High-voltage injuries require C-spine immobilization and rhabdomyolysis monitoring with serial CK/myoglobin. Lightning-strike victims need neuro re-exams and ophthalmology referral.

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-5 mL/kg IV over 10-20 min for raised ICP; may infuse 0.1-1 mL/kg/hr continuous
20-40 mg/kg IV load (max 1500 mg) for seizure prophylaxis in severe TBI (GCS <8)
1-2 mcg/kg IV for sedation/analgesia; continuous infusion 1-3 mcg/kg/hr in ventilated patients
For GCS <9: Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV; avoid hypoxia and hypotension

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min for acute herniation; hold if serum osm >320
5-15 mg/kg IV load then 1-3 mg/kg/hr infusion for refractory ICP; requires EEG monitoring
20 mg/kg IV load (max rate 1 mg/kg/min) if levetiracetam unavailable; monitor ECG

notes: Maintain CPP 40-50 mmHg; avoid hypotension, hypoxia, and hyperthermia. Place ICP monitor for GCS <8 after resuscitation; target PaCO2 35-39 mmHg.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds TBI 3rd Ed, CHOP TBI Pathway 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 WVU guideline updated levetiracetam as preferred seizure prophylaxis over phenytoin

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid IV bolus; repeat up to 3 boluses then consider blood products
10-20 mL/kg IV if hemorrhagic shock unresponsive to 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid; use O-neg if uncrossmatched
15-20 mg/kg IV (max 1 g) over 10 min within 3h of injury; then 2 mg/kg/hr infusion for 8h
1-2 mcg/kg IV q30-60min PRN or intranasal for trauma pain; avoid sedation in unprotected airway

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV for RSI or procedural sedation; maintains hemodynamic stability
pRBC:FFP:Platelets in 1:1:1 ratio for life-threatening hemorrhage; activate early
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if vasopressor needed after adequate volume resuscitation

notes: Follow ATLS primary survey (ABCDE) adapted for pediatric anatomy. Hypotension is a late sign in children; tachycardia and poor perfusion are early indicators of shock.
source: ATLS 10th Ed, CHOP Trauma Resuscitation Pathway 2024

diagnosis_name: Open wound of unspecified body region

first_line_drugs.notes: Lidocaine 4%-Epinephrine 0.1%-Tetracaine 0.5% gel; apply to wound edges 20-30 min before repair
Local infiltration 1-2 mg/kg (max 4.5 mg/kg); buffer with NaHCO3 (1:10 ratio) to reduce pain
Copious NS irrigation under pressure (18G syringe); minimum 200 mL for contaminated wounds
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; add TIG 250 units IM if dirty wound and incomplete series

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-octyl cyanoacrylate (Dermabond) for clean linear lacerations <5 cm; not for high-tension areas
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6-8h for 5-7 days if high infection risk (bite, crush, contaminated)
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h for bite wounds; 5-7 day course

notes: Use absorbable sutures (chromic gut/poliglecaprone) in children to avoid traumatic removal. Assess tetanus status, neurovascular integrity, and tendon function before closure.
source: CHOP Laceration Pathway 2024, Texas Children's Wound Care 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 TCH guideline updated to prefer absorbable deep sutures (poliglecaprone 5-0/6-0)

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (glucose <60 mg/dL); recheck glucose in 15 min
50-100 mg/kg IV (max 4 g/day) if meningitis suspected; give within 1h of suspicion
20 mg/kg IV q8h for suspected HSV encephalitis; continue pending PCR results

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q6h (max 60 mg/kg/day) added to ceftriaxone for suspected bacterial meningitis
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid toxicity suspected; repeat q2-3min as needed
0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) if seizure activity suspected; repeat once after 5 min if needed
0.01 mg/kg IV (max 0.2 mg) over 15 sec if benzodiazepine ingestion; avoid if seizure hx

notes: Use AEIOU-TIPS mnemonic for differential. Check glucose on every pediatric AMS patient; obtain CT head, CBC, BMP, ammonia, toxicology screen, and blood culture early.
guideline_changes: 2024 Iberoamerican Academy published comprehensive pediatric AMS treatment guide

first_line_drugs.notes: For GCS <9: Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV; use cuffed ETT; verify with ETCO2
5 mL/kg IV over 10-20 min if signs of herniation; monitor Na q4-6h, target Na <160
2-5 mL/kg IV if glucose <60 mg/dL; maintain normoglycemia 80-180 mg/dL

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min for acute herniation; hold if serum osm >320 mOsm/L
20-40 mg/kg IV load (max 1500 mg) for seizure prophylaxis or treatment
20 mg/kg IV q8h if viral encephalitis suspected; pending LP and PCR
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid toxicity suspected; may repeat q2-3min

notes: Secure airway immediately for GCS <9. Order CT head, full metabolic panel, ammonia, toxicology screen, and continuous EEG monitoring in PICU.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds 3rd Ed, PALS/AHA 2025
guideline_changes: 2025 PALS updated to recommend cuffed ETT for all pediatric intubations

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-5 mL/kg IV bolus over 10-20 min; then 0.1-1 mL/kg/hr continuous infusion; target Na 150-160
Elevate head of bed 30 degrees; keep head midline to optimize venous drainage
1-3 mcg/kg/hr IV continuous infusion in intubated patients; minimize ICP spikes from stimulation

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min q4-6h; hold if serum osmolality >320 or signs of renal failure
5-15 mg/kg IV load then 1-3 mg/kg/hr for refractory ICP; requires continuous EEG and arterial line
Surgical option for refractory ICP not responsive to maximal medical therapy
Target ETCO2 30-35 mmHg as temporizing measure for acute herniation only; avoid routine use

notes: Maintain CPP >=40 mmHg (target 40-50 mmHg). Monitor serum Na closely with HTS; RCT showed HTS superior to mannitol with ICP reduction of -14.3 vs -5.4 mmHg.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds 3rd Ed, Neurocritical Care Society 2020
guideline_changes: Hypertonic saline now preferred over mannitol; RCT shows superior ICP reduction and lower mortality

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mL/kg IV slow push over 3-5 min (max 30 mL); cardiac membrane stabilizer; onset 1-3 min
0.1 units/kg IV bolus (max 10 units) with Dextrose 10% 5 mL/kg; onset 15-30 min; monitor glucose
2.5-5 mg nebulized q20min x3 doses or 4 mcg/kg IV over 20 min; shifts K+ intracellularly
1-2 mEq/kg IV over 5-10 min if concurrent metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2); not first-line alone

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 g/kg PO or PR (max 15 g/dose in children); onset 1-2 hr; avoid in bowel obstruction
4-8.4 g PO daily in older children; newer potassium binder with better GI tolerability
1-2 mg/kg IV if adequate renal function; enhances renal K+ excretion; onset 15-30 min
For refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >7 with ECG changes) or renal failure; most definitive therapy

notes: Treat K+ >6.5 mEq/L or any level with ECG changes emergently. Give calcium first if ECG changes present; insulin-glucose is most reliable shift therapy.
guideline_changes: 2024 Janjua review updated pediatric hyperkalemia management algorithm

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mL/kg IV bolus over 10-20 min for severe symptomatic hyponatremia; raise Na 3-5 mEq/L acutely
Free water deficit replacement IV over 48-72h; correct Na no faster than 0.5 mEq/L/hr or 10-12 mEq/day
20 mL/kg IV bolus if hypovolemic; then switch to appropriate correction fluid

second_line_drugs.notes: Free water replacement for pure water deficit; infuse slowly to avoid cerebral edema
For euvolemic/hypervolemic hyponatremia (SIADH); restrict to 50-75% maintenance
1-2 mg/kg IV for hypervolemic hyponatremia; promotes free water excretion

notes: Maximum Na correction: 8 mEq/L in 24h for chronic hyponatremia to prevent osmotic demyelination. For hypernatremia, correct slowly over 48-72h to prevent cerebral edema.
guideline_changes: 2024 NEJM Evidence debate on safe correction limits; current consensus maintains 8 mEq/L/day max

first_line_drugs.notes: Identify and treat etiology: DKA, sepsis, renal failure, poisoning; acidosis often self-corrects
20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration/hypovolemia-related acidosis; reassess after each bolus
0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr IV continuous infusion; do NOT bolus in pediatric DKA; target glucose drop 50-100 mg/dL/hr

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mEq/kg IV slow infusion ONLY if pH <6.9-7.0; avoid in DKA unless pH <6.9; risk of hypokalemia
Alternative buffer if NaHCO3 contraindicated; 3-5 mL/kg IV; use with caution in neonates
For severe refractory acidosis or toxic alcohol ingestion; corrects acidosis and removes toxin

notes: Avoid routine bicarb in pediatric patients; evidence shows harm in most settings. Focus on treating the underlying cause; check anion gap, lactate, ketones, and toxicology.
guideline_changes: 2024 real-world study shows NaHCO3 benefit only when Cl >=113 mmol/L; harmful when Cl <107

first_line_drugs.notes: Vt 6-8 mL/kg IBW; plateau pressure <=28 cmH2O; driving pressure <=15 cmH2O; titrate PEEP per FiO2 table
16+ hr/day for severe PARDS (OI >16); initiate within first 3 days; monitor hemodynamics
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/hr IV continuous infusion; target RASS -1 to -2 for ventilator synchrony

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 ppm for refractory hypoxemia; assess response at 4h; bridge to ECMO if no improvement
1-2 mg/kg/day IV for >7 days in unresolving PARDS; extrapolated from adult ARDS data
Consider if failing conventional ventilation; mean airway pressure +5 above conventional
Cisatracurium 1-4 mcg/kg/min IV infusion for severe PARDS with ventilator dyssynchrony

notes: Use PALICC-2 criteria for diagnosis. Target Vt 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight; consider ECMO referral for OI >40 refractory to optimal management.
guideline_changes: PALICC-2 (2023) updated PARDS diagnostic criteria and recommended lung-protective bundles

first_line_drugs.notes: Immediate BVM with 100% O2; rate 20-30 breaths/min with advanced airway; ensure chest rise
Cuffed ETT preferred; size (age/4)+3.5; RSI with Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV
0.01 mg/kg IV/IO (1:10,000) q3-5min if progresses to pulseless arrest; max 1 mg/dose
0.02 mg/kg IV (min 0.1 mg, max 0.5 mg) for bradycardia-associated respiratory arrest

second_line_drugs.notes: LMA as rescue airway if intubation fails; size per weight-based chart
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid-induced respiratory arrest; may repeat q2-3min
4 mL/kg ET for neonates with respiratory failure from surfactant deficiency

notes: Most pediatric cardiac arrests are secondary to respiratory failure. Verify ETT with waveform capnography; aim ETCO2 35-40 mmHg post-intubation.
guideline_changes: 2025 AHA updated pediatric ventilation rate to 20-30/min with advanced airway and recommends cuffed ETT

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion; first-line inotrope; SUCRA 85% in 2024 meta-analysis
0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min IV (load 50 mcg/kg over 15 min optional); reduces afterload + inotropy
1-2 mg/kg IV q6-12h for pulmonary edema/fluid overload; monitor electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion; alternative inotrope if epinephrine unavailable
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV if vasodilatory component present; SUCRA 80% in meta-analysis
0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV for duct-dependent cardiac lesions in neonates
Consider for refractory cardiogenic shock unresponsive to maximal vasoactive support

notes: Avoid fluid boluses in cardiogenic shock unless clear evidence of hypovolemia. Milrinone preferred for post-surgical low cardiac output; dopamine no longer recommended first-line.
source: SSC Peds 2020, ACC Cardiogenic Shock 2025, PICU Network Meta-Analysis 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 network meta-analysis ranks epinephrine over dopamine; milrinone preferred for low CO states

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus over 5-20 min; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; reassess after each
10-20 mL/kg IV if hemorrhagic shock and no response to 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid; use O-neg if urgent
15-20 mg/kg IV (max 1 g) over 10 min within 3h of trauma; then 2 mg/kg/hr for 8h

second_line_drugs.notes: pRBC:FFP:Plt at 1:1:1 ratio for massive hemorrhage; activate when >40 mL/kg blood products predicted
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if persistent hypotension after adequate volume; bridge therapy only
0.5 mL/kg IV slow push during massive transfusion to correct citrate-induced hypocalcemia

notes: Tachycardia and poor perfusion are early signs; hypotension is a late and ominous finding in children. Delay intubation if possible until volume resuscitated to avoid hemodynamic collapse.
source: ATLS 10th Ed, SSC Peds 2020, Dutch Pediatric Society CPG

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; assess for fluid overload
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if fluid-refractory shock; initiate within 1h if poor perfusion persists
50-100 mg/kg IV (max 4 g/day) as empiric antibiotic; give within 1h of sepsis recognition
15 mg/kg IV q6h if MRSA suspected; adjust for renal function; target trough 10-20 mcg/mL

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV as alternative first-line vasopressor; may be preferred in cold shock
2 mg/kg IV bolus (max 100 mg) then 1-2 mg/kg/day for catecholamine-resistant shock
20-40 mg/kg IV q8h (max 2 g/dose) for broad-spectrum coverage if resistant organisms suspected

notes: Use Phoenix Sepsis Score >=2 for diagnosis (replaces SIRS). Antibiotics within 1 hour; vasopressors after 40-60 mL/kg fluids; can give peripheral vasopressors if no central line.
guideline_changes: 2024 Phoenix Sepsis Score replaces SIRS-based criteria for pediatric sepsis diagnosis

diagnosis_name: Systemic inflammatory response syndrome of infectious origin with organ failure

first_line_drugs.notes: Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg IV + Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV within 1h; adjust per culture results
10-20 mL/kg IV bolus; up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; monitor for fluid overload signs
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV for fluid-refractory shock; titrate to MAP appropriate for age

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV for cold shock with low cardiac output phenotype
2 mg/kg IV bolus (max 100 mg) then divided q6h for catecholamine-resistant shock
20-40 mg/kg IV q8h (max 2 g/dose) if ESBL or multidrug-resistant organisms suspected
1 g/kg IV over 12h; consider for toxic shock syndrome or streptococcal sepsis

notes: Phoenix Sepsis Score >=2 now defines pediatric sepsis with organ dysfunction. Prioritize early antibiotics, aggressive but monitored fluid resuscitation, and early vasoactive support.
source: SSC Peds 2020, Phoenix Sepsis Criteria 2024, SCCM PANDEM 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 Phoenix criteria replaced SIRS-based definitions; PANDEM guidelines published for PICU care

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by antiepileptic, sedative-hypnotic and antiparkinsonism drugs

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) if within 1-2h of ingestion; multi-dose 0.5 g/kg q4h for carbamazepine/phenobarbital
Intubate for GCS <9 or loss of protective reflexes; RSI with Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV
20 mL/kg bolus for hypotension; maintain euvolemia; monitor glucose and electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg IV (max 0.2 mg) over 15 sec for pure BZD overdose; repeat q60sec up to 0.05 mg/kg or 1 mg total
1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus for QRS widening from carbamazepine/TCA; target serum pH 7.45-7.55
For severe phenobarbital toxicity (level >100 mcg/mL) or refractory carbamazepine poisoning
1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min for lipophilic drug toxicity with cardiovascular collapse

notes: Avoid flumazenil in mixed overdose or chronic benzodiazepine use (seizure risk). Multi-dose activated charcoal is effective for phenobarbital, carbamazepine, and phenytoin.

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by diuretics and other unspecified drugs, medicaments and biological substances

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration/hypotension; repeat as needed; correct electrolyte abnormalities
1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) if within 1-2h of ingestion and patient is alert with intact airway
KCl 0.5-1 mEq/kg IV over 1h for hypokalemia; MgSO4 25-50 mg/kg IV for hypomagnesemia

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL/kg IV slow push if symptomatic hypocalcemia or hyperkalemia (from K-sparing diuretics)
Norepinephrine 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV for refractory hypotension despite fluid resuscitation

notes: Diuretic toxicity causes electrolyte derangements (hypoK, hypoNa, hypoMg) and dehydration. Monitor ECG for arrhythmias; effects may be delayed 2-4h until diuretic action begins.
source: AACT Position Statements, Poison Center Guidelines

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by nonopioid analgesics, antipyretics and antirheumatics

first_line_drugs.notes: IV: 150 mg/kg over 1h, then 15 mg/kg/hr continuous; PO: 140 mg/kg load, then 70 mg/kg q4h x17 doses
1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) within 1-4h of acetaminophen ingestion; most effective within 1h
Maintenance fluids; correct dehydration; monitor hepatic and renal function q6-12h

second_line_drugs.notes: PEG 25 mL/kg/hr via NG for sustained-release preparations or massive NSAID ingestion
1-2 mEq/kg IV for salicylate poisoning; target urine pH 7.5-8.0 for enhanced elimination
For severe salicylate poisoning (level >100 mg/dL, renal failure, or refractory acidosis)

notes: Start NAC within 8h of acetaminophen ingestion for best efficacy; use Rumack-Matthew nomogram for risk assessment. For salicylate poisoning, alkalinize urine and monitor serial levels.
guideline_changes: New consensus guidelines released for acetaminophen poisoning management with updated NAC protocols

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV loading dose, then 10 mg/kg IV q12h until alcohol level <30 mg/dL; safe in pediatrics
2-5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (common in pediatric ethanol ingestion); monitor glucose q1h
20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration; maintain euvolemia; correct metabolic acidosis

second_line_drugs.notes: 7.6 mL/kg loading dose IV, then 0.8-1.3 mL/kg/hr infusion; if fomepizole unavailable
For methanol/ethylene glycol with severe acidosis, visual changes, renal failure, or level >50 mg/dL
1-2 mg/kg IV q4-6h (max 50 mg) for methanol poisoning; enhances formate metabolism
Thiamine 100 mg IV + Pyridoxine 50 mg IV for ethylene glycol; cofactors for detoxification

notes: Fomepizole blocks alcohol dehydrogenase, preventing toxic metabolite formation. Start before lab confirmation if high suspicion; early treatment may obviate need for hemodialysis.
guideline_changes: Fomepizole now preferred over ethanol infusion as antidote for toxic alcohols in pediatrics

first_line_drugs.notes: Nothing by mouth; assess airway for edema/stridor; intubate early if oropharyngeal burns or stridor
20 mL/kg IV bolus for shock; maintain euvolemia; correct acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities
Omeprazole 1 mg/kg IV q12-24h (max 40 mg) to reduce acid-mediated injury to damaged mucosa

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for pain; avoid oral medications until endoscopy completed
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy for all symptomatic patients to grade injury; guides further management
50-100 mg/kg IV if evidence of perforation or mediastinitis; broad-spectrum coverage

notes: Do NOT induce vomiting, give activated charcoal, or attempt neutralization. Endoscopy within 12-24h for symptomatic children; asymptomatic patients may be observed 2-4h and discharged.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.02-0.05 mg/kg IV q5-10min; double dose until secretions dry; may need very large cumulative doses
25-50 mg/kg IV over 30 min (max 1 g); then 10-20 mg/kg/hr infusion; give within 24-36h of exposure
Remove clothing, wash skin with soap and water; activated charcoal 1 g/kg PO if oral ingestion within 1h

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) for seizures from organophosphate poisoning; repeat q5-10min PRN
0.01-0.02 mg/kg IV as alternative to atropine; does not cross BBB so won't treat CNS symptoms
4-8 mg/kg IV load then 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr; alternative oxime used in some regions

notes: Organophosphate poisoning causes cholinergic crisis (SLUDGE/DUMBELS). Titrate atropine to secretion drying, not pupil size; pralidoxime must be given early before aging occurs.
source: WHO Organophosphate Management, CDC ATSDR Guidelines

diagnosis_name: Toxic effect of venom of contact with venomous animals

first_line_drugs.notes: 4-6 vials IV initial dose (same as adult); 8-12 vials for severe; repeat 4-6 vials q6h x3 for control
20 mL/kg IV bolus for hypotension; aggressive fluid resuscitation for anaphylaxis or venom shock
0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1,000) max 0.3 mg for antivenom anaphylaxis; repeat q5-15min PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 vials IV initial dose for pit viper envenomation; longer half-life, fewer recurrent symptoms
10-15 mL/kg IV for severe coagulopathy (fibrinogen <100, platelets <50K) unresponsive to antivenom
1 mg/kg IV (max 50 mg) premedication or for mild antivenom reactions; give with H2 blocker
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; recommended for all envenomation wounds

notes: Children receive larger venom load relative to body size and are at higher morbidity risk. Antivenom dose is based on venom amount (not patient weight); serial limb measurements q15-60 min.
source: ACS Snakebite Guidelines, Texas Children's Envenomation Protocol

first_line_drugs.notes: 8 mg/m2/day PO divided TID; stress dose: triple during illness; max 20 mg/m2/day
0.05-0.2 mg PO daily for mineralocorticoid replacement; monitor renin and electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-3 mg/m2/day PO daily; not preferred in children due to growth suppression
50-100 mg/m2 IV bolus for adrenal crisis then 50-100 mg/m2/24h continuous infusion

notes: Avoid long-acting glucocorticoids in growing children; use lowest dose allowing adequate growth. All patients need emergency injection training and steroid sick-day rules.
guideline_changes: NICE 2024 guideline published; Khindivi oral HC solution FDA-approved 2025

diagnosis_name: Congenital adrenogenital disorders associated with enzyme deficiency

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/m2/day PO divided TID; titrate to lowest dose controlling androgens and allowing growth
0.1-0.2 mg PO daily for salt-wasting forms; add NaCl 2-4 g/day in infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-100 mg PO BID based on weight (>=4y); adjunct to GC to reduce steroid dose
2-4 mg/m2/day PO; only in post-pubertal patients when HC is insufficient

notes: Hydrocortisone is first-line in growing children; avoid dexamethasone pre-puberty. Crinecerfont (2024) enables GC dose reduction by blocking excess ACTH-driven androgen production.
guideline_changes: Crinecerfont (Crenessity) FDA-approved Dec 2024 as first-in-class CRF1 antagonist adjunct for CAH age >=4y

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mL/kg IV bolus (0.2 g/kg); then infusion 2-5 mg/kg/min to maintain glucose >70 mg/dL
5-15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hyperinsulinism; max 15 mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/day SC divided TID-QID; second-line if diazoxide-unresponsive
0.02-0.03 mg/kg IV/IM/SC (max 1 mg); for emergency use or continuous IV 5-10 mcg/kg/hr

notes: Identify and treat underlying cause; diazoxide is first-line medical therapy for congenital hyperinsulinism. Monitor for diazoxide side effects including fluid retention and hypertrichosis.
guideline_changes: Low-dose diazoxide protocols (2-5 mg/kg/day) supported for transient hyperinsulinism (2024 data)

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 U/kg/hr IV; start after 1h fluids and K+ >3.0; do NOT bolus
10-20 mL/kg IV bolus over 20-30 min; then replace deficit over 36h with added KCl 40 mmol/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.15 U/kg SC q1-2h; for mild DKA without IV access in resource-limited settings
0.5-1 mmol/kg IV over 6h; if phosphate <1 mg/dL; use with KCl to provide total K+ replacement

notes: Start insulin only after initial fluid resuscitation and confirmed K+ >3.0 mmol/L. Monitor for cerebral edema especially in children <5y; avoid rapid glucose drops >5 mmol/L/hr.
guideline_changes: ISPAD 2024 updated DKA chapter; low-dose insulin 0.05 U/kg/hr now preferred initial rate

diagnosis_name: Disorders of branched-chain amino-acid metabolism and fatty-acid metabolism

first_line_drugs.notes: Restrict leucine to age-appropriate tolerance; use MSUD-specific formula to maintain Leu <200 umol/L (<5y)
High-calorie IV glucose (8-10 mg/kg/min) during acute decompensation to promote anabolism

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-300 mg/day PO trial for thiamine-responsive MSUD variants; assess response over 3 weeks
50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for fatty acid oxidation defects; max 3 g/day
1-2 g/kg/day PO for long-chain FAO defects; avoid fasting >4-6h in infants

notes: Acute metabolic crises require emergency IV glucose and protein restriction; hemodialysis if leucine >1500 umol/L. Liver transplant is curative for classic MSUD.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.58 mg/kg (100 U/kg) IV weekly over 3-4h; premedicate with antipyretic/antihistamine
0.5 mg/kg IV weekly over 1-3h; monitor for infusion reactions in ~20% patients

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg IV weekly over 4h; for Maroteaux-Lamy syndrome
Hematopoietic stem cell transplant; preferred for severe MPS I (Hurler) if <2.5y with DQ >70

notes: ERT does not cross the blood-brain barrier; HSCT is preferred for MPS I Hurler phenotype if done early. Early diagnosis via newborn screening improves outcomes substantially.
guideline_changes: Gene therapy trials advancing for MPS I/II; intrathecal ERT for CNS involvement under study

first_line_drugs.notes: 100-500 mg/day PO trial; ~50% of CBS-deficient HCU patients are B6-responsive
Use met-free amino acid formula; target plasma homocysteine <50 umol/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-125 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for B6-nonresponsive HCU; max 6 g/day in children
Folate 1-5 mg/day PO + B12 1 mg/day PO as adjunct to lower homocysteine

notes: Test all patients for pyridoxine responsiveness before committing to lifelong dietary restriction. Early treatment prevents intellectual disability, thromboembolism, and lens dislocation.
guideline_changes: Novel enzyme replacement therapies (pegtibatinase, pegtarviliase) in clinical trials for CBS-deficient HCU

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 g/kg PO q4-6h (older children); maintains euglycemia in GSD I/III; avoid in <1y
Complex carbs q2-4h daytime; continuous NG glucose infusion overnight for infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hyperuricemia in GSD I; max 800 mg/day
1-2 g/kg PO at bedtime; provides longer glucose release than raw cornstarch; age >5y

notes: Frequent feeding and cornstarch are the mainstay for hepatic GSD; avoid fasting >4h in infants. Monitor lactate, uric acid, lipids, and liver size regularly.

first_line_drugs.notes: Limit phenylalanine intake using Phe-free amino acid formula; target blood Phe 120-360 umol/L
10-20 mg/kg/day PO once daily; for BH4-responsive patients (~25-50%); trial response required

second_line_drugs.notes: SC injection; approved for adults/adolescents >=16y; titrate from 2.5 mg to 20-40 mg daily; anaphylaxis risk
Supplement PO to compete with Phe transport across BBB; adjunct in patients with poor dietary compliance

notes: Newborn screening enables early dietary treatment preventing intellectual disability. All patients should undergo BH4 responsiveness testing; diet remains the cornerstone of therapy.
guideline_changes: ACMG 2024 evidence-based guideline published Aug 2024; sapropterin conditionally recommended in pregnancy

diagnosis_name: Sphingolipidosis and other lipid storage disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 30-60 U/kg IV every 2 weeks; for Gaucher type 1; adjust based on clinical response
Gaucher: 100 mg PO TID (adults); NPC: substrate reduction therapy per weight-based dosing

second_line_drugs.notes: PO per weight-based dosing for Niemann-Pick type C; first FDA-approved NPC therapy (2024)
PO weight-based dosing for NPC; second FDA-approved NPC therapy (Sep 2024)
60 U/kg IV every 2 weeks for Gaucher type 1 (age >=4y); alternative to imiglucerase

notes: ERT is effective for Gaucher type 1 but not for neuronopathic forms. Two new NPC therapies approved in 2024 represent major advances for this previously untreatable condition.
guideline_changes: Arimoclomol (Miplyffa) and levacetylleucine (Aqneursa) both FDA-approved Sep 2024 for Niemann-Pick C

first_line_drugs.notes: Intensive health behavior and lifestyle treatment (26+ contact hours); diet, physical activity, behavioral therapy
SC weekly; titrate 0.25mg to 2.4mg over 16wk; age >=12y with BMI >=95th percentile
SC daily; titrate 0.6mg to 3.0mg over 4wk; age >=12y (6-12y per recent trial data)

second_line_drugs.notes: 120 mg PO TID with meals; age >=12y; add multivitamin due to fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption
Per weight-based dosing PO daily; age >=12y; monitor HR and mood

notes: Semaglutide achieves up to 17% BMI reduction, the most potent of approved agents. Pharmacotherapy is adjunct to, not replacement for, intensive lifestyle modification.
guideline_changes: Semaglutide FDA-approved Dec 2022 for age >=12y; liraglutide expanded to ages 6-12y (2024 trial data)

diagnosis_name: Protein-energy malnutrition of moderate and mild degree

first_line_drugs.notes: Energy-dense supplementary food 250-500 kcal/day added to usual diet; attend checkup q2wk for 4-6 wk
Multivitamin/mineral supplement per WHO; include iron, zinc, folate, vitamin A per local protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: 92 g/day sachet (~500 kcal) for moderate acute malnutrition in 6-59 month olds
1-1.5 kcal/mL enteral formula for older children; increase by 10-20% q2-3d as tolerated

notes: Counsel on age-appropriate feeding practices and complete immunizations. Monitor weight-for-height z-score every 2 weeks; refer if no improvement after 4-6 weeks.
guideline_changes: WHO 2023 guideline expanded to include moderate wasting management in 6-59 month age group

first_line_drugs.notes: 75 kcal/100 mL; stabilization phase days 1-7; feed q2-3h; low protein/Na to prevent refeeding syndrome
150-200 kcal/kg/day in rehab phase; 10-15 kg total over 6-8 wk; for age >=6mo without complications
40 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for 5-7 days; routine antibiotic per WHO SAM protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 kcal/100 mL; transition/rehabilitation phase; increase feeds by 10 mL/feed until desired intake
5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (glucose <54 mg/dL); then feed immediately

notes: Start refeeding cautiously with F-75 to avoid refeeding syndrome; monitor electrolytes (K, Mg, PO4) BID for 4 days. Treat hypothermia, hypoglycemia, and dehydration before addressing nutrition.
guideline_changes: WHO 2023 guideline consolidated wasting and oedema management; community-based RUTF approach strengthened

first_line_drugs.notes: Start at 50-80% estimated caloric needs for current weight; advance 10-20%/day if electrolytes stable
100-300 mg IV/PO daily before and during refeeding to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Check K, Mg, PO4 BID for >=4 days; replace aggressively; do not advance feeds if abnormal

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mmol/kg/day IV or PO if PO4 low; critical to prevent refeeding syndrome cardiac complications
Polymeric 1 kcal/mL formula via NG if unable to eat; advance slowly per refeeding protocol

notes: High refeeding syndrome risk in any child malnourished >10 days; start calories low and advance slowly. Correct electrolytes before advancing nutritional support.

first_line_drugs.notes: Age 6-11y <30kg: ELX50/TEZ25/IVA37.5 AM + IVA75 PM; >=30kg or >=12y: ELX100/TEZ50/IVA75 AM + IVA150 PM; with fat
2.5 mg nebulized daily; for age >=6y to improve mucus clearance and lung function

second_line_drugs.notes: For gating mutations; age 1mo-5y: 25-75 mg PO BID by weight; >=6y: 150 mg PO q12h with fat
500-2500 lipase U/kg/meal PO; max 10,000 U/kg/day; for pancreatic insufficiency
4 mL nebulized BID; for age >=6y; improves mucociliary clearance

notes: Trikafta has transformed CF care with significant lung function improvement and reduced mortality. Start CFTR modulators at earliest eligible age to attenuate disease progression.
guideline_changes: Trikafta approved down to age 2y (2023); Canadian consensus recommends starting CFTR modulators at youngest age possible

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for mitochondrial disorders; higher doses not necessarily more effective
50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for carnitine deficiency or secondary deficiency; max 3 g/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-400 mg/day PO for Complex I/II defects and MADD; assess response over 3 months
5-10 mg/kg/day PO as mitochondrial antioxidant adjunct

notes: Metabolic disorders require individualized cocktail therapy targeting specific enzyme defects. Evidence for mitochondrial supplements is limited but CoQ10 and carnitine are widely used.
guideline_changes: FDA granted Rare Pediatric Disease designation for BPM31510IV for primary CoQ10 deficiency (2024)

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg IM monthly for 3-6 months for pubertal induction; start at chronological age >=14y and BA >=12y
0.25-0.5 mg PO daily (or 5 mcg/kg/day) for pubertal induction; start at chronological age >=13y and BA >=11y

second_line_drugs.notes: Start 6.25 mcg (1/8 of 50 mcg patch) nightly; increase q6mo; more physiologic than oral
2.5 mg PO daily for 6 months in boys; aromatase inhibitor to boost growth and pubertal tempo

notes: Distinguish constitutional delay from permanent hypogonadism with clinical follow-up and labs. Low-dose sex steroid trial for 3-6 months can help differentiate and provide psychological benefit.
guideline_changes: 2024 JCRPE review updated delayed puberty management perspectives including aromatase inhibitor use

first_line_drugs.notes: 7.5-15 mg IM q28d (1-mo depot) or 11.25-30 mg IM q3mo; US doses 200-300 mcg/kg/28d
50 mg SC implant replaced annually; avoids repeat injections; minor procedure under local anesthesia

second_line_drugs.notes: 22.5 mg IM q24wk (6-mo depot, Triptodur); or 3.75 mg IM q28d
45 mg SC q6mo; small-volume injection; FDA-approved for CPP

notes: GnRH agonists are standard of care for central precocious puberty to preserve adult height potential. Monitor for adequate LH suppression and growth velocity deceleration.
guideline_changes: 6-month depot formulations (Fensolvi SC, Triptodur IM) now widely used reducing injection burden

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mcg/kg/day SC daily for GHD; up to 67 mcg/kg/day for ISS; titrate by IGF-1 levels

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.16 mg/kg SC once weekly; long-acting GH approved for pediatric GHD; reduces injection burden
0.66 mg/kg SC once weekly; alternative long-acting GH for pediatric GHD

notes: Individualize dosing based on growth response and IGF-1 levels; stop GH when growth velocity falls below 2.5 cm/year. Once-weekly formulations (2024) improve adherence.
guideline_changes: Somapacitan (Sogroya) once-weekly GH approved for new pediatric indications (2024)

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 10-15 mcg/kg/day PO; 1y: 4-6 mcg/kg/day; adolescents: 2-4 mcg/kg/day; take on empty stomach

second_line_drugs.notes: Same mcg/kg dosing as tablet; use liquid formulation in neonates/infants unable to swallow tablets

notes: Start treatment within 2 weeks of life for congenital hypothyroidism to prevent cognitive impairment. Recheck TSH/FT4 at 4-6 weeks after any dose change.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day PO divided TID initially; maintenance 0.2 mg/kg/day; treat >=3 years
0.5-2 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for symptom control (tachycardia, tremor) while awaiting MMI effect

second_line_drugs.notes: Dose calculated per thyroid size; for children >10y failing ATD or with significant adverse effects
Near-total thyroidectomy by experienced surgeon; for large goiter, non-compliance, or ATD failure

notes: Methimazole is first-line; never use propylthiouracil (PTU) in children due to hepatotoxicity risk. Treatment duration of 3-5+ years recommended; euthyroid usually achieved in 4-6 weeks.

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 0.5-1 mg IM at birth; VKDB treatment: 1-5 mg IV/SC; preterm: 0.3-0.5 mg/kg IM
1000 mcg PO daily x4mo for nutritional deficiency; or 1000 mcg IM weekly x4wk then monthly

second_line_drugs.notes: 100-300 mg PO daily for scurvy treatment; continue for 1-3 months until stores replete
25-200 IU/day PO for deficiency; higher doses in cholestasis or fat malabsorption

notes: Vitamin K prophylaxis at birth prevents hemorrhagic disease of newborn. Identify and treat underlying cause of deficiency (dietary, malabsorption, or chronic disease).

first_line_drugs.notes: <6mo: 50,000 IU PO; 6-12mo: 100,000 IU PO; >12mo: 200,000 IU PO; day 1, day 2, then repeat at 2 wk

second_line_drugs.notes: 100,000 IU PO q4-6mo for ages 6-11mo; 200,000 IU PO q4-6mo for 12-59mo in endemic areas

notes: Active corneal xerophthalmia is a medical emergency requiring immediate high-dose vitamin A. Also give vitamin A with measles treatment per WHO protocol regardless of deficiency status.

first_line_drugs.notes: <1y: 2000 IU/day PO x12wk; >1y: 3000-6000 IU/day PO x8-12wk; recheck 25-OH-D after course
30-75 mg/kg/day elemental calcium PO divided TID for rickets with low Ca or inadequate dietary intake

second_line_drugs.notes: 50,000 IU PO weekly x6-8wk for adolescents; then maintenance 600-1000 IU/day
0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/day PO for vitamin D-dependent rickets type I or renal disease

notes: All breastfed infants should receive 400 IU/day vitamin D prophylaxis from birth. Double treatment dose in patients with obesity, malabsorption, or dark skin pigmentation.
source: AAP 2008, Indian Academy of Pediatrics 2021, Endocrine Society 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h; 5 mL q5min initially, increase as tolerated
20 mL/kg IV bolus for severe dehydration; repeat up to 3 boluses if needed
20 mL/kg IV bolus for severe dehydration; alternative to NS

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) to facilitate oral rehydration
Nasogastric ORS at 20 mL/kg/h if PO fails and IV not available

notes: Oral rehydration is first-line for mild-moderate dehydration; IV reserved for severe or failed ORT. Continue breastfeeding; avoid sugary fluids.
guideline_changes: Emphasis on hypoosmolar ORS and early refeeding confirmed in 2023 updates

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for mild-mod dehydration
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) for 10-14 days
0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for persistent vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID for up to 7 days; antisecretory
250-750 mg/day PO for 5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic
10 billion CFU/day PO for 5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic

notes: Antibiotics not routinely indicated; reserve for specific pathogens or toxic/invasive presentations. Early refeeding with regular diet improves outcomes.
guideline_changes: 2023 WHO update reinforces zinc supplementation and reduced-osmolarity ORS

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for dehydration management
Identify and eliminate offending allergens; trial of hypoallergenic formula in infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for vomiting
1.5 mg/kg PO TID for up to 7 days as antisecretory agent
10 billion CFU/day PO for 5-7 days as adjunctive therapy

notes: Focus on identifying underlying cause (food allergy, eosinophilic GI disease). Supportive care with rehydration and dietary modification is mainstay.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day PO daily x5 days (max 500 mg/day) for Campylobacter/Shigella
50-75 mg/kg/day IV/IM q24h (max 2 g) for invasive Salmonella or severe cases
Rehydration first-line; antibiotics only for invasive/toxic presentations

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) x5-7 days if susceptible
8 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID x5 days if susceptible
30 mg/kg/day PO divided TID x10 days for C. difficile

notes: Most bacterial gastroenteritis is self-limited; antibiotics reserved for invasive disease, immunocompromised, or specific pathogens. Always check local resistance patterns.
guideline_changes: 2024 ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN H. pylori update emphasizes susceptibility-guided therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: 12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg/day PO days 2-5 (max 250 mg)
50 mg/kg/day IV/IM q24h (max 2 g) x5 days for resistant or severe cases

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) x3 days if susceptible
8 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID x5 days if susceptible
20 mg/kg PO QID x5 days (max 400 mg/dose); alternative per WHO

notes: Always obtain stool culture and susceptibility before or concurrent with empiric therapy. XDR strains increasing; ceftriaxone preferred if resistance suspected.
guideline_changes: CDC 2023 alert on increasing XDR Shigella strains; azithromycin resistance rising

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for mild-mod dehydration
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) x10-14 days
0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for persistent vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID x5-7 days; antisecretory adjunct
250-750 mg/day PO x5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic

notes: No antiviral therapy available; treatment is entirely supportive. Rotavirus vaccination remains the most effective prevention strategy.

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g/dose) preop and for complicated cases
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 10 mg/kg IV q8h (max 500 mg)
Laparoscopic appendectomy; standard of care for confirmed appendicitis

second_line_drugs.notes: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate then PO x7-10 days for uncomplicated; 58-75% 1yr success
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) for severe allergy or resistant organisms
15 mg/kg IV q12h (max 1 g/day) for complicated cases with penicillin allergy

notes: Uncomplicated appendicitis may be managed non-operatively with antibiotics in select cases, but surgery remains standard. Complicated appendicitis with abscess may benefit from initial antibiotics and interval appendectomy.
guideline_changes: 2024 pathways increasingly support non-operative antibiotic-first for uncomplicated cases

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g/dose) perioperative
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 10 mg/kg IV q8h (max 500 mg)
Laparoscopic preferred; open if needed. Postop abx based on intraop findings

second_line_drugs.notes: 45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 875 mg/dose) for step-down oral therapy
Cipro 10 mg/kg PO BID + Metro 10 mg/kg PO TID for oral step-down if pen-allergic

notes: If unspecified, obtain imaging (US or CT) to classify. Gangrenous/perforated appendicitis requires 7-10 days of antibiotics; uncomplicated needs only perioperative doses.

first_line_drugs.notes: Soave, Swenson, or Duhamel; single-stage preferred. Laparoscopic or transanal approach
20 mL/kg NS via rectal catheter BID-TID preoperatively to decompress

second_line_drugs.notes: Staged approach for severe enterocolitis or total colonic aganglionosis
30 mg/kg/day IV/PO divided TID x7-14 days for Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis
Intrasphincteric injection for postop obstructive symptoms; 1-4 units/kg per session

notes: Definitive treatment is surgical. Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) is a life-threatening complication requiring urgent rectal irrigations and IV antibiotics.
guideline_changes: 2023 NASPGHAN guidance on postsurgical defecation issues; trend toward single-stage pull-through

first_line_drugs.notes: Insert NG tube for suction; strict NPO. Fluid resuscitate with NS/LR 20 mL/kg boluses
NS or LR 20 mL/kg bolus; correct electrolytes. Maintenance 1.5x calculated rate
Emergency laparotomy/laparoscopy for strangulation, volvulus, or failed conservative Rx

second_line_drugs.notes: Therapeutic contrast enema for meconium ileus or adhesive partial obstruction
100 mg/kg/dose IV q8h (max 4.5 g) if peritonitis or perforation suspected

notes: Etiology must be urgently identified (volvulus, hernia, adhesions, intussusception). Midgut volvulus requires emergent surgery without delay for imaging.

first_line_drugs.notes: Pneumatic reduction under fluoroscopy; 83-97% success rate. Max pressure 120 mmHg
NS 20 mL/kg bolus; correct dehydration before reduction attempt

second_line_drugs.notes: US-guided saline enema reduction; 73-92% success. No radiation exposure
Operative manual reduction or resection if enema fails, peritonitis, or perforation
0.5 mg/kg IV single dose (max 10 mg); may reduce recurrence rate (adjunctive)

notes: Air enema is first-line for ileocolic intussusception without peritonitis. Recurrence rate is 5-10%; lead points more common in children >2 years requiring surgical evaluation.
guideline_changes: 2024 meta-analysis confirms air enema superiority over hydrostatic reduction in success rates

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided QD-BID (max 40 mg/day) x8-12 weeks
1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg); FDA-approved for pediatric erosive esophagitis
88-440 mcg swallowed BID (age-based); for eosinophilic esophagitis

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg PO BID (mixed with sucralose); for eosinophilic esophagitis
SC q2-4wk weight-based dosing; for EoE refractory to PPI/steroids, age >=1yr
2-food or 6-food empiric elimination diet for EoE; dietitian-guided

notes: Distinguish reflux esophagitis (PPI-responsive) from eosinophilic esophagitis (may need topical steroids or biologics). EGD with biopsy required for EoE diagnosis.
guideline_changes: PPI-nonresponsive EoE no longer a diagnostic category; dupilumab approved for EoE age 1+ (2022)

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 40 mg/day) x4-8 weeks for acid suppression
50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g/dose) x14 days; part of H. pylori triple therapy
15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; if susceptible

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; if clarithromycin-resistant
Quadruple therapy component; age >12yr, 262 mg PO QID x14 days
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose); mucosal protectant

notes: H. pylori testing is indicated for ulcers/erosions. Susceptibility testing is mandatory per 2024 guidelines before initiating eradication therapy. Non-H. pylori gastritis managed with PPI alone.
guideline_changes: 2024 update mandates susceptibility-guided therapy; high-dose PPI + amoxicillin-based triple therapy x14 days

first_line_drugs.notes: Upright positioning post-feed; smaller/frequent feeds; thickened feeds in infants
1 mg/kg/day PO QD-BID (max 20 mg BID) x4-8 weeks; if lifestyle changes fail
0.7-1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg); for erosive GERD

second_line_drugs.notes: Famotidine 0.5 mg/kg PO BID (max 20 mg BID); H2RA if PPI not tolerated
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 30 mg); alternative PPI
Laparoscopic antireflux surgery for refractory GERD with complications

notes: Avoid acid suppression in infants with extraesophageal symptoms only. PPI trial of 4-8 weeks recommended for typical GERD symptoms before further workup.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO QD-BID (max 40 mg/day) x4-8 weeks for ulcer healing
50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g/dose) x14 days as part of triple therapy
15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; susceptibility-guided

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg) x8 weeks; alternative PPI
0.5 mg/kg PO BID (max 20 mg BID) if PPI contraindicated/not tolerated
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose); cytoprotective adjunct

notes: Test for H. pylori in all pediatric peptic ulcers; eradication confirmed 4-8 weeks post-treatment. NSAID-related ulcers require NSAID cessation and PPI therapy.
guideline_changes: 2024 H. pylori guidelines emphasize 14-day susceptibility-guided triple therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Rest, adequate hydration, balanced nutrition. No specific antiviral therapy available
10-15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day) for fever/pain; AVOID if hepatotoxicity concern
NS/D5NS maintenance if unable to tolerate PO; correct dehydration from vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO q8h (max 4 mg/dose) for nausea/vomiting
240 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 4 g/dose) for severe pruritus

notes: Self-limited in children with excellent prognosis; fulminant hepatitis rare (<1%). Avoid hepatotoxic medications; no dietary restrictions needed. HAV vaccination prevents disease.

first_line_drugs.notes: Rest, hydration, nutrition. Most acute HBV is self-limited in older children
NS maintenance for dehydration; monitor glucose, coagulation, LFTs serially

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.015 mg/kg PO QD (max 0.5 mg) for severe/protracted acute HBV or fulminant hepatitis
8 mg/kg PO QD (max 300 mg) age >=2yr; for severe acute HBV (off-label)

notes: Acute HBV rarely needs antivirals; interferon is contraindicated in acute phase. Neonates require HBIG + HBV vaccine within 12h of birth to prevent vertical transmission.
guideline_changes: 2024 WHO expanded treatment criteria for chronic HBV; EASL 2025 updated management guidance

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based: <17kg 150/37.5mg, 17-<30kg 200/50mg, >=30kg 400/100mg PO QD x12wk
Weight-based granules or tablets PO QD x8wk; pangenotypic, age >=3yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based PO QD x12wk; for genotypes 1,4,5,6; age >=3yr
No DAAs approved <3yr; observe for spontaneous clearance (high in infants)

notes: Treat all children >=3yr with chronic HCV; spontaneous clearance high in infants so defer treatment. SVR rates >95% with current DAA regimens in children.
guideline_changes: Pan-genotypic DAAs now FDA-approved for children >=3yr; child-friendly formulations available

first_line_drugs.notes: Etiology-specific: antivirals for HBV/HCV, chelation for Wilson, UDCA for cholestasis
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for cholestatic liver diseases
High-calorie diet 120-150% RDA; MCT-enriched formula; fat-soluble vitamin supplementation

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 100 mg) for ascites management
1-2 mg/kg/day PO/IV (max 6 mg/kg/day) for ascites; combine with spironolactone
Definitive treatment for decompensated cirrhosis or liver failure

notes: Management is etiology-directed. Monitor for complications: variceal bleeding, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome. Liver transplant evaluation should be early for decompensation.
guideline_changes: Resmetirom (THR-beta agonist) approved 2024 for MASH in adults; pediatric trials pending

first_line_drugs.notes: 150 mg/kg IV over 1h, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h for APAP toxicity
Continuous infusion to maintain glucose >70 mg/dL; 6-8 mg/kg/min glucose infusion rate
2-10 mg IV single dose for coagulopathy; repeat if INR remains elevated

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV q8h x14-21 days if HSV hepatitis suspected (neonates especially)
2 mg/kg/day PO/IV (max 60 mg) for autoimmune hepatitis-related ALF
10-15 mL/kg IV for active bleeding with coagulopathy; avoid routine use
Emergency listing for ALF meeting King's College or PELD criteria

notes: Transfer to pediatric liver transplant center urgently. NAC beneficial for APAP-induced ALF; role in non-APAP PALF remains uncertain. Treat identifiable causes (HSV, Wilson, AIH).
guideline_changes: 2024 ISPGHAN consensus on pediatric ALF management; NAC role debated for non-APAP PALF

first_line_drugs.notes: Ceftriaxone 50-75 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 30 mg/kg/day IV divided TID
US-guided needle aspiration (<5cm) or catheter drainage (>5cm)

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g) for polymicrobial pyogenic abscess
35-50 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided TID x7-10 days for amoebic liver abscess
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) for resistant organisms or treatment failure
Open drainage for ruptured abscess, peritonitis, or failed percutaneous approach

notes: Pyogenic abscesses require both antibiotics and drainage; amoebic abscesses often resolve with metronidazole alone. Antibiotic duration 2-6 weeks depending on response.
source: Pediatric Surgery 2023, Johns Hopkins ABX Guide 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured diet + exercise program; target 7-10% weight loss if overweight/obese
Reduce sugar-sweetened beverages, processed foods; increase fiber and whole foods
400-800 IU/day PO for biopsy-proven NASH in children without diabetes

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for cholestatic liver disorders
500 mg PO BID (age >=10yr) for NAFLD with insulin resistance; limited evidence

notes: NAFLD/MASLD is the most common pediatric chronic liver disease. Screen overweight children with ALT; liver biopsy for indeterminate cases. Lifestyle modification is primary treatment.
guideline_changes: NAFLD renamed MASLD/MASH; resmetirom approved 2024 for adult MASH; pediatric guidance forthcoming

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) x2-4wk, then taper over 6-8wk to 2.5-5 mg/day maintenance
1-2.5 mg/kg/day PO QD (added after initial steroid response); steroid-sparing agent

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g BID) if azathioprine intolerant/refractory
3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; bridge therapy for severe AIH or steroid-refractory
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; target trough 3-5 ng/mL for refractory AIH

notes: Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is the most common inflammatory liver disease requiring treatment. Relapse rate 60-80% after withdrawal; many patients need lifelong immunosuppression.
guideline_changes: EASL 2025 updated AIH guidelines; mycophenolate increasingly used as steroid-sparing agent

first_line_drugs.notes: For esophageal varices with bleeding or high-risk stigmata; repeat q2-4wk until obliteration
1-2 mcg/kg IV bolus then 1-2 mcg/kg/h infusion (max 50 mcg/h) for acute variceal bleed
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; titrate to 25% HR reduction. Limited pediatric evidence

second_line_drugs.notes: Endoscopic injection for variceal bleeding if banding not feasible (younger children)
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 100 mg) for ascites
Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt for refractory bleeding/ascites
Surgical bypass for extrahepatic portal vein obstruction in eligible children

notes: Beta-blockers for primary prophylaxis lack robust pediatric evidence; band ligation preferred for secondary prophylaxis. Evaluate for liver transplant if decompensated cirrhosis.
guideline_changes: Baveno VII criteria for non-invasive assessment of portal hypertension; beta-blockers debated in children

first_line_drugs.notes: Lifelong elimination of wheat, barley, rye. Dietitian-guided. Labeled GF oats permitted
Iron, folate, calcium, vitamin D, zinc as needed based on deficiency labs

second_line_drugs.notes: Short-term lactose avoidance if secondary lactase deficiency present at diagnosis
1-2 mg/kg/day PO x2-4wk for celiac crisis with severe malabsorption (rare)

notes: GFD is the only proven treatment. Monitor tTG-IgA for adherence. Follow-up with dietitian and serology at 3-6 month intervals initially, then annually.
guideline_changes: 2024 ESPGHAN position paper on GFD implementation; oats allowed if labeled gluten-free

first_line_drugs.notes: High-calorie diet 120-150% RDA; oral supplements or enteral nutrition as needed
Iron, calcium, zinc, magnesium, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) based on labs
Gluten-free diet for celiac, PERT for pancreatic insufficiency, antibiotics for SIBO

second_line_drugs.notes: TPN for severe malabsorption/intestinal failure; individualized caloric and micronutrient goals
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/dose PO (max 2 mg/dose) for symptomatic diarrhea; age >2yr only
240 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for bile acid malabsorption
