notes: Thorough workup essential to identify cause (celiac, IBD, EPI, SIBO, cow milk protein allergy). Prompt referral to pediatric GI for growth failure associated with malabsorption.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1.5-2x maintenance with D5 0.9% NS; bolus 20 mL/kg if hemodynamically unstable
15 mg/kg PO/IV q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); first-line analgesia
0.5 mg/kg IV q6h (max 30 mg/dose) x5 days for pain; NSAID adjunct
Begin PO/NG feeds within 24-48h if tolerated; low-fat diet not required

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg IV q2-4h (max 10 mg) for severe pain unresponsive to non-opioids
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) ONLY for infected necrosis; prophylactic abx NOT recommended
For biliary pancreatitis with choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology

notes: Aggressive IV hydration and early enteral feeding are cornerstones of treatment. Prophylactic antibiotics not indicated. Opioids are safe and should not be withheld for pain.
guideline_changes: 2024 BSPGHAN guidance published; liberal IV fluids associated with improved outcomes in pediatric AP

first_line_drugs.notes: 500-2500 lipase units/kg/meal PO; 250-500 LU/kg/snack. Enteric-coated capsules
Vitamins A, D, E, K at 1-2x RDA PO daily; monitor levels q6-12 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO QD to enhance PERT efficacy by reducing gastric acid inactivation
Medium-chain triglycerides 1-2 mL/kg/day PO for improved fat absorption
ERCP for chronic pancreatitis with duct stones, strictures, or pseudocysts

notes: PERT is the mainstay for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Do not exceed 10,000 LU/kg/day to avoid fibrosing colonopathy. Monitor growth and nutritional status regularly.
guideline_changes: 2024 European guidelines on EPI diagnosis and PERT dosing published (UEG/EPC/ESPGHAN)

first_line_drugs.notes: Gentle digital reduction with lubrication; apply sugar/salt to edematous mucosa before reduction
0.4-0.8 g/kg/day PO QD (max 17 g) to prevent straining
Screen for cystic fibrosis, treat constipation, parasites, malnutrition

second_line_drugs.notes: Submucosal injection of 30% saline or 5% phenol above dentate line; outpatient procedure
For recurrent prolapse after failed conservative Rx; age >4yr or chronic predisposition
Anal encirclement suture for recurrent prolapse in select patients

notes: Self-limiting in 90% of children <3 years with conservative management. All children with rectal prolapse should be tested for cystic fibrosis (sweat chloride test).

first_line_drugs.notes: Magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine + Maalox +/- lidocaine) swish-spit q4-6h for pain
15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day) for pain and fever
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day) for pain and inflammation; age >6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO 4x/day (max 800 mg) x7 days for herpetic gingivostomatitis
15 mL swish-spit BID x10 days; prevent secondary infection of ulcers
Apply to ulcers TID-QID after meals; for recurrent aphthous stomatitis
1-5 mL PO swish-spit QID x7-14 days for oral candidiasis

notes: Identify etiology: viral (HSV, enterovirus), aphthous, fungal, or trauma-related. Maintain hydration; soft/bland diet. Herpetic stomatitis most common in children 1-5 years.

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-12 sessions over 3 months; strongest evidence for functional abdominal pain disorders
8-12 sessions; addresses pain catastrophizing, improves coping and functioning
187 mg PO TID (age >8yr) for IBS-related pain; smooth muscle relaxant

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg PO QHS (max 25 mg); neuromodulator for chronic functional pain
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (max 12 mg); for functional dyspepsia/abdominal migraine
10 billion CFU/day PO; modest evidence for functional abdominal pain

notes: Most pediatric chronic abdominal pain is functional (Rome IV criteria). Red flags (weight loss, GI bleeding, fever) require workup. Gut-brain psychotherapy is first-line per 2025 guidelines.
guideline_changes: 2025 ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN IBS/FAP guidelines published; gut-brain psychotherapies have strongest evidence

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for dehydration; ad libitum for maintenance
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) x10-14 days per WHO guidelines
Continue breastfeeding; regular diet. Reduce juice/sugary drinks for toddler diarrhea

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID x5-7 days; antisecretory for acute watery diarrhea
0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO TID (max 2 mg/dose); chronic diarrhea only, age >2yr
250-750 mg/day PO x5-7 days as adjunct; evidence for duration reduction

notes: Investigate persistent (>14 days) or chronic (>4 weeks) diarrhea for underlying cause. Toddler diarrhea is most common chronic cause in ages 1-3yr and is managed with dietary changes.

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 20 mL/kg bolus; crossmatch blood. Transfuse pRBC 10-15 mL/kg if Hb <7 or unstable
<20kg: 10 mg IV daily; 20-55kg: 20 mg IV daily; >55kg: 40 mg IV daily; for upper GI bleed
1-2 mcg/kg IV bolus then 1-2 mcg/kg/h infusion (max 50 mcg/h) for variceal hemorrhage

second_line_drugs.notes: EGD within 12h for variceal bleed or 24h for non-variceal; band ligation or injection
15-25 mg/kg IV q6-8h (max 1 g/dose) as adjunct for severe hemorrhage
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose) for mucosal protection

notes: Stabilize hemodynamics first. Upper GI bleed (hematemesis, melena) requires IV PPI and early endoscopy. Lower GI bleed: consider Meckel scan in young children, colonoscopy in older children.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg); may repeat q8h. For AGE-related vomiting
5 mL PO q2-5min after vomiting; increase as tolerated to prevent dehydration

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg PO/IM/IV q4-6h (max 25 mg); age >2yr. Sedation risk
1.25 mg/kg PO/IV q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for motion sickness or vestibular vomiting
0.1-0.15 mg/kg PO/IV q6-8h (max 10 mg); limited use due to EPS risk
0.15 mg/kg IV q6h (max 10 mg) for CINV or cyclic vomiting prophylaxis

notes: Ondansetron is first-line for AGE-related vomiting; single dose usually sufficient. Rule out surgical emergencies (pyloric stenosis, malrotation, obstruction) before attributing to benign cause.

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured behavioral therapy; weekly sessions for 12-16 weeks; caregiver-focused
Individual or group CBT; weekly sessions targeting anger management and problem-solving skills
0.25-0.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.5-3 mg/day in 2 divided doses; ages 5-17

second_line_drugs.notes: 300 mg BID-TID; target serum level 0.6-1.2 mEq/L; monitor renal/thyroid; ages 7+
2 mg/day initial, titrate to 5-15 mg/day; ages 6-17; monitor metabolic
Intensive community-based family therapy; 3-5 months duration; for severe cases

notes: Always treat comorbid ADHD/depression first. Pharmacotherapy only after psychosocial interventions fail; short-term antipsychotic use recommended.
guideline_changes: Network meta-analysis confirms SGAs most efficacious for disruptive behaviors

diagnosis_name: Disorders of social functioning with onset specific to childhood and adolescence

first_line_drugs.notes: Graduated exposure-based CBT; weekly sessions 12-20 weeks; for selective mutism and social anxiety
For reactive attachment disorder (F94.1); 12-25 sessions; caregiver involvement essential
Primary intervention for RAD/DSED; establish safe consistent caregiving environment

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-40 mg/day; off-label for selective mutism; ages 5+
12.5-25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; off-label for social anxiety; ages 6+
Child-centered or directive play therapy; weekly sessions; for younger children unable to do CBT

notes: No FDA-approved medications for RAD; stable caregiving is cornerstone. For selective mutism, CBT with graduated exposure is first-line; SSRIs adjunctive.
guideline_changes: Updated AACAP anxiety guideline now covers selective mutism under anxiety spectrum

diagnosis_name: Emotional disorders with onset specific to childhood

first_line_drugs.notes: Individual or group CBT with exposure; 12-16 weekly sessions; ages 6-18; CAMS-supported
10 mg/day initial, titrate to max 20 mg/day; ages 7+; for separation/generalized anxiety
25 mg/day initial (6-12y), 50 mg/day (12+); titrate to max 200 mg/day; off-label for anxiety

second_line_drugs.notes: 25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; FDA-approved for pediatric OCD ages 8+
30 mg/day initial, max 60-120 mg/day; FDA-approved for GAD ages 7+
5 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-20 mg/day; ages 12+; off-label for anxiety
1-3 mg/kg/day in divided doses; last-line for refractory separation anxiety; ECG monitoring

notes: Combination CBT+SSRI yields ~81% improvement vs 55% SSRI alone (CAMS trial). Benzodiazepines not recommended for pediatric anxiety.
guideline_changes: 2020 AACAP guideline recommends combined CBT+SSRI for moderate-severe anxiety

first_line_drugs.notes: Habit reversal + relaxation training; 8 sessions over 10 weeks; ages 9+; AAN Level B
0.05 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.1-0.4 mg/day in divided doses; monitor BP; ages 6+
0.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 1-4 mg/day; extended-release preferred; monitor BP

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/day initial, titrate to 2-20 mg/day; for moderate-severe tics; monitor metabolic
0.5-1 mg/day initial, titrate to 1.5-10 mg/day; typical antipsychotic; monitor for EPS
25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; AAN Level C evidence; monitor cognition
12.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 25-75 mg/day; for refractory tics; monitor for depression

notes: Treat comorbid ADHD/OCD first as they often cause more impairment than tics. Stimulants generally safe and do not worsen tics.
guideline_changes: 2025 reaffirmation emphasizes CBIT as first-line behavioral intervention for ages 9+

first_line_drugs.notes: Multidisciplinary evaluation + therapy; birth-3 years; federally mandated; individualized IFSP
1-3 sessions/week; targeting expressive/receptive language milestones; ages 0-5
1-3 sessions/week; fine motor, sensory processing, ADL skills; individualized goals
1-3 sessions/week; gross motor milestones (sitting, crawling, walking); ages 0-5

second_line_drugs.notes: Comprehensive evaluation for underlying etiology; genetic/metabolic workup as indicated
10-25 hrs/week; for children with concurrent ASD features; evidence-based behavioral approach
3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron if deficient; iron deficiency linked to developmental delays

notes: Critical intervention window is before age 3. Most delays resolve with early intervention; refer immediately upon identification.
source: AAP 2022 Milestone Update, IDEA Part C
guideline_changes: CDC/AAP 2022 updated milestone checklists to improve early detection sensitivity

diagnosis_name: Pervasive developmental disorders / autism spectrum disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-40 hrs/week intensive or 10-25 hrs/week focused; individualized; ages 2+; gold standard
1-5 sessions/week; targeting functional communication; may include AAC devices
1-3 sessions/week; sensory integration, fine motor, self-care skills; individualized
0.25 mg/day initial (<20kg) or 0.5 mg/day (>20kg); titrate to 0.5-3 mg/day; FDA-approved 5-16y for irritability

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/day initial, titrate to 5-15 mg/day; FDA-approved ages 6-17 for irritability in ASD
1 mg/day initial, titrate to 1-4 mg/day; for hyperactivity/impulsivity in ASD; off-label
0.3 mg/kg/dose initial, titrate to max 1 mg/kg/dose; for comorbid ADHD symptoms; lower doses in ASD
2.5-5 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-40 mg/day; for anxiety/repetitive behaviors in ASD; off-label

notes: Behavioral and developmental therapies are primary treatment; medications target co-occurring symptoms, not core ASD. Monitor weight at baseline and quarterly on antipsychotics.
guideline_changes: 2024 guidelines expand pharmacologic options for co-occurring irritability and emphasize early ABA

diagnosis_name: Specific developmental disorders of scholastic skills

first_line_drugs.notes: Orton-Gillingham-based phonics instruction; 4-5 sessions/week; 30-60 min; individualized
School-based specialized instruction; legally mandated accommodations under IDEA; annual review
1-3 sessions/week; phonological awareness, reading fluency; for language-based learning disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 sessions/week; handwriting, visual-motor integration; for dysgraphia component
Text-to-speech, audiobooks, speech-to-text software; classroom accommodations
Structured math tutoring for dyscalculia; multisensory approaches; 3-5 sessions/week

notes: No pharmacologic treatment for learning disabilities; medications only for comorbid ADHD/anxiety. Early identification before age 7 yields best outcomes.
guideline_changes: Emphasis on structured literacy and Orton-Gillingham approaches for dyslexia intervention

diagnosis_name: Encopresis not due to a substance or known physiological condition

first_line_drugs.notes: Disimpaction: 1-1.5 g/kg/day PO x3-6 days; Maintenance: 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day PO; mix in 6-8 oz fluid
Scheduled sitting 5-10 min after meals; positive reinforcement; stool diary; ongoing

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mL/kg/day PO in divided doses; osmotic laxative; for PEG-intolerant patients
Ages 2-6: 2.5-5 mL/day PO; Ages 6-12: 5-10 mL/day PO; stimulant laxative; short-term
1-3 mL/kg/day PO; max 240 mL/day; ages 1+; not for children at aspiration risk
1 pediatric suppository PR daily as needed; for acute relief; ages 2+

notes: Disimpaction must precede maintenance therapy. Taper laxatives by 25% every 2-3 months over 6-12 months once behavioral modification established.
guideline_changes: PEG 3350 dosing optimized in 2024 studies confirming 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day maintenance range

first_line_drugs.notes: Moisture-sensing alarm; use nightly for 12-16 weeks minimum; best sustained response; 66% cure rate
120 mcg melt tablet SL 30-60 min before bed; may increase to 240-360 mcg; restrict fluids 8 hrs

second_line_drugs.notes: For refractory cases; alarm nightly + desmopressin 120-240 mcg SL at bedtime; 12-16 weeks
5 mg PO at bedtime; for enuresis with daytime urgency/frequency; anticholinergic; ages 5+
25 mg PO at bedtime (ages 6-12); max 50 mg (ages 12+); last-line due to cardiac risk; ECG required

notes: Active treatment from age 6. Treat constipation and daytime incontinence first. Structured desmopressin withdrawal over weeks reduces relapse.
guideline_changes: 2025 IPSA consensus recommends structured desmopressin withdrawal to improve cure rates

first_line_drugs.notes: Dietitian-led caloric needs assessment; age-appropriate dietary plan; weekly weight monitoring
High-calorie diet 120-150% of RDA; fortified foods; frequent small meals; adjust to age
Age-appropriate daily multivitamin with iron and zinc; correct specific deficiencies identified on labs

second_line_drugs.notes: PediaSure/Ensure 1-3 servings/day as caloric supplement; 240 kcal per 8 oz; ages 1-13
2 mg PO BID-TID (ages 2-6) or 4 mg PO BID-TID (ages 7+); appetite stimulant; off-label
For severe malnutrition or oral intake failure; continuous or bolus feeds; inpatient or home

notes: Investigate underlying etiology (malignancy, celiac, IBD, eating disorders, psychosocial). Weight loss >5% in children warrants urgent workup.

first_line_drugs.notes: Target 120-150% estimated energy requirement; calorie-dense foods; fortify breast milk/formula
For infants: assess latch, increase feeds; concentrate formula to 24-30 kcal/oz if needed
Registered dietitian evaluation; structured meal schedule; eliminate juice/low-calorie fillers
Daily supplementation; iron 2-6 mg/kg/day if deficient; zinc 1 mg/kg/day if deficient; PO

IV fluid resuscitation; TPN until enteral feeding established

second_line_drugs.notes: Topical silver sulfadiazine/povidone-iodine; epithelialization for giant defects
Perioperative broad-spectrum coverage; ampicillin + gentamicin

notes: Giant omphalocele (>5 cm) often requires staged approach. Screen for Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and cardiac anomalies.

first_line_drugs.notes: Fistula ligation + esophageal anastomosis via right thoracotomy
Continuous suction of upper pouch; head-up position to prevent aspiration
NPO with IV fluids and TPN until postoperative feeding

second_line_drugs.notes: PPI or H2 blocker post-repair; GER very common after TEF repair
Feasible but technically challenging; higher leak rates in neonates

notes: Urgent but not emergent; stabilize first. 50% have VACTERL association; obtain cardiac echo, renal US, spine imaging.

first_line_drugs.notes: OG/NG tube feeding for preterm <32-34 wks unable to coordinate suck-swallow
Add HMF when volume reaches 100 mL/kg/day in preterm infants
D10W + electrolytes; TPN if enteral feeds not tolerated >48-72h

second_line_drugs.notes: Feeding therapy and oral stimulation program for dysphagia
24 kcal/oz preterm formula if mother's milk unavailable

notes: Prioritize mother's own milk; human milk reduces NEC risk. Advance feeds by 10-30 mL/kg/day as tolerated.

first_line_drugs.notes: Large-bore NGT to continuous or intermittent suction; strict NPO
NS 10-20 mL/kg bolus; then maintenance + deficit replacement
Laparotomy for mechanical obstruction; urgency depends on etiology

second_line_drugs.notes: Gastrografin enema; diagnostic and therapeutic for meconium ileus
Mucolytic via NGT or enema for meconium ileus; 2-4% solution
Broad-spectrum: ampicillin + gentamicin if perforation suspected

notes: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is obstruction until proven otherwise. Obtain upright abdominal XR and surgical consult urgently.

first_line_drugs.notes: Ampicillin 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h + Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV q24-48h
15 mg/kg IV load, then 7.5 mg/kg IV q12h for anaerobic coverage
Bowel rest 7-14 days; NGT decompression; TPN for nutrition

second_line_drugs.notes: Laparotomy or peritoneal drain for perforation (pneumoperitoneum)
15 mg/kg IV q8-12h if MRSA or late-onset concern; adjust per culture
Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium for VLBW NEC prevention

notes: Stage with Bell criteria. Pneumoperitoneum is absolute surgical indication. Human milk feeding significantly reduces NEC incidence.
guideline_changes: Probiotics increasingly recommended for NEC prevention in VLBW infants; lactoferrin under study

first_line_drugs.notes: Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via LISA or ETT within 2h of birth for RDS
Loading 20 mg/kg IV/PO, then 5-10 mg/kg/day maintenance for apnea prevention
Start CPAP 6-8 cmH2O from birth; avoid intubation if possible
Betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 doses 24h apart to mother before delivery

second_line_drugs.notes: Start amino acids Day 0 at 2-3 g/kg/day; lipids Day 1 at 1-3 g/kg/day
0.1 mg/kg IV q24h x3 for IVH prevention in <28 wk; debated

notes: Active resuscitation now considered from 22 weeks at proactive centers. Thermoregulation with polyethylene wrap critical at delivery.
guideline_changes: LISA (less invasive surfactant) emerging as preferred delivery method for surfactant

first_line_drugs.notes: Check blood glucose within 2h of birth, then q3-6h for 24h; feed early
Initiate breastfeeding within 1h of birth; supplement if BG <45 mg/dL
Skin-to-skin contact; servo-controlled warmer; target axillary 36.5-37.4C

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg bolus then 4-6 mg/kg/min GIR if BG <25 mg/dL or symptomatic
Check venous Hct at 4-6h; PET if Hct >65% with symptoms

notes: High risk for hypoglycemia, polycythemia, hypothermia, and feeding intolerance. Monitor closely for first 24-48 hours.

first_line_drugs.notes: Screen BG at 2h of age; if <25 mg/dL refeed and recheck in 1h
Breastfeed or formula within 1h of birth; q2-3h feeds for first 24h

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg bolus then GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min for persistent hypoglycemia
0.5 mL/kg buccal for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia; massage into mucosa

notes: LGA infants of diabetic mothers have highest hypoglycemia risk. Screen glucose for 12h; monitor for birth injuries from macrosomia.

first_line_drugs.notes: Continuous skin-to-skin contact; improves thermoregulation and BF
Mother's own milk preferred; start trophic feeds 10-20 mL/kg/day
Maintain 36.5-37.4C; use warmer/incubator for infants <1500g

second_line_drugs.notes: Start if enteral feeds not tolerated; amino acids 2-3 g/kg/day from Day 0
2-4 mg/kg/day elemental iron from 2-6 weeks of age

notes: WHO recommends immediate KMC for all LBW infants regardless of stability. Monitor glucose, temperature, and weight gain closely.
guideline_changes: WHO 2022 updated guidelines emphasizing kangaroo mother care immediately after birth

first_line_drugs.notes: Screen BG at 2h of birth; q3-6h for 24h if at risk for hypoglycemia
Initiate breastfeeding within 1h; monitor for adequate intake
Observe for meconium aspiration, polycythemia, hypoglycemia signs

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W infusion if symptomatic hypoglycemia; GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min
Venous Hct at 4-6h of life; treat if >65% with symptoms

notes: Post-term neonates at higher risk for meconium aspiration, hypoglycemia, and polycythemia. Close monitoring first 24-48h.

first_line_drugs.notes: Nasal CPAP 5-8 cmH2O from delivery room; avoid intubation if possible
Loading 20 mg/kg IV/PO, maintenance 5-10 mg/kg/day for apnea of prematurity
Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg if FiO2 >0.3 on CPAP; early rescue preferred

second_line_drugs.notes: Early parenteral nutrition; protein 2-3 g/kg/day, lipids 1-3 g/kg/day from Day 0-1
2-4 mg/kg/day from 2-6 weeks of age; continue through first year
400 IU/day once on full enteral feeds; for bone health

notes: Delayed cord clamping >60 seconds recommended. Minimize handling, maintain thermoregulation, and promote human milk feeding.
guideline_changes: LISA increasingly adopted; delayed cord clamping (>60s) now standard

first_line_drugs.notes: BG at 2h, then q3-6h for 24h; target >45 mg/dL after 4h of life
Breastfeed within 1h; supplement with expressed milk or formula if needed
Skin-to-skin or servo-controlled warmer; SGA infants are hypothermia-prone

second_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg bolus then GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min if BG persistently <45
Venous Hct at 4-6h; PET if symptomatic with Hct >65%

notes: SGA neonates at risk for hypoglycemia, polycythemia, and hypothermia. Monitor feeding adequacy and weight gain closely.

first_line_drugs.notes: Start Day 0: amino acids 2-3 g/kg/day, lipids Day 1 1-3 g/kg/day, D10W
Mother's own milk 10-20 mL/kg/day from Day 1; advance 20-30 mL/kg/day
Loading 20 mg/kg, maintenance 5-10 mg/kg/day for apnea prevention

second_line_drugs.notes: Add at 100 mL/kg/day enteral volume; human milk-based preferred for VLBW
250-400 IU/kg SC 3x/week with iron; limited benefit, ROP risk debated
2-4 mg/kg/day elemental iron from 2 weeks of age

notes: Prioritize human milk to reduce NEC. Minimize blood draws, use delayed cord clamping, and target restrictive transfusion thresholds.
guideline_changes: Human milk-based fortifier shown to reduce NEC vs bovine-based in VLBW

first_line_drugs.notes: Irradiance >=30 mcW/cm2/nm; start when TSB at or above threshold for GA/age
0.5-1 g/kg IV over 2h for isoimmune hemolytic disease if TSB approaching exchange level

second_line_drugs.notes: Double-volume (160 mL/kg); when TSB exceeds exchange threshold despite phototherapy
D10W + electrolytes at 1.5x maintenance as supplement to feeds during phototherapy

notes: Rh and ABO incompatibility most common causes. Check DAT, reticulocyte count, and serial bilirubin. IVIG may reduce need for exchange.
guideline_changes: AAP 2022 raised phototherapy thresholds; IVIG role more selective

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg IV/SC for active bleeding; 0.3-0.5 mg/kg if <1500g birth weight
0.5-1 mg IM at birth for all neonates; single dose prevents VKDB

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg IV for moderate-severe active bleeding with coagulopathy
2 mg PO at birth, 2-4 wks, and 6-8 wks if parents decline IM; less reliable

notes: IM vitamin K at birth is the standard of care for VKDB prevention. Late VKDB (2-12 weeks) occurs with missed or oral-only prophylaxis.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg over 2-4h; use restrictive thresholds (Hb 7-10 based on support)
2-4 mg/kg/day elemental iron from 2-6 weeks in preterm infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-400 IU/kg SC 3x/week; modest reduction in late transfusions; ROP concern
60-120 seconds at birth; reduces anemia and transfusion need in preterms

notes: Minimize phlebotomy losses. Restrictive transfusion thresholds are safe per ETTNO/TOP trials. Delayed cord clamping is standard.
guideline_changes: Restrictive transfusion thresholds (Hb 7-10 g/dL based on age/support) validated by ETTNO/TOP trials

first_line_drugs.notes: Increase fluid intake; NS bolus 10-20 mL/kg for mild symptomatic polycythemia
PET with NS; Vol = (Hct observed - 50)/Hct observed x 90 mL/kg x wt(kg)

second_line_drugs.notes: Monitor Hct q6-12h; many cases resolve with hydration alone

notes: PET for Hct >70% asymptomatic or >65% symptomatic. Use venous (not capillary) Hct for decision-making. Long-term benefit of PET unproven.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg; threshold <25,000/mcL (restrictive) per PlaNeT-2 trial
Antibiotics for sepsis, maternal ITP workup; most cases self-limiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 g/kg IV prior to platelet transfusion for alloimmune thrombocytopenia (NAIT)
10-15 mL/kg at <50,000/mcL only for active bleeding or pre-procedure

notes: Restrictive threshold (25K) is safer than liberal (50K) per PlaNeT-2 trial. For NAIT, use HPA-matched or washed maternal platelets.
guideline_changes: PlaNeT-2 trial established restrictive platelet transfusion threshold of 25,000/mcL

first_line_drugs.notes: 16 mg/kg PO q12h for 6 months; start within first month of life
6 mg/kg IV q12h; for life-threatening disease, switch to oral when stable

second_line_drugs.notes: For asymptomatic cCMV or mild symptoms; serial hearing tests recommended

notes: Monitor CBC weekly (neutropenia common side effect). Treatment improves hearing outcomes if started within 1 month of life.
guideline_changes: 6 months valganciclovir now standard for symptomatic cCMV; oral preferred over IV for non-critical

first_line_drugs.notes: Multidisciplinary: cardiology, ophthalmology, audiology, neurology assessments
Repair of PDA or other CHD when indicated; timing based on cardiac status

second_line_drugs.notes: Early audiology referral; hearing aids or cochlear implant for SNHL
Ophthalmology referral; lens aspiration if visually significant cataract

notes: No specific antiviral treatment. Infants shed virus for months; isolate and enforce hand hygiene. Screen glucose and thyroid function.

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO x2 days, then 1 mg/kg/day x2-6 mo, then 1 mg/kg 3x/wk to 12 mo
100 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h for 12 months
10 mg PO 3x/week throughout pyrimethamine therapy; protects bone marrow

second_line_drugs.notes: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day if CSF protein >1 g/dL or severe chorioretinitis

notes: Treat for 12 months minimum. Monitor CBC weekly while on daily pyrimethamine due to bone marrow suppression.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5% ointment prophylaxis to both eyes within 1h of birth
25-50 mg/kg IM/IV x1 dose (max 125 mg) for gonococcal conjunctivitis
50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h x14 days for chlamydial conjunctivitis

second_line_drugs.notes: Frequent saline eye lavage for gonococcal conjunctivitis as adjunct
20 mg/kg/day PO x3 days for chlamydial; if erythromycin fails

notes: Gonococcal ophthalmia is a medical emergency requiring systemic antibiotics. Topical treatment alone insufficient for chlamydial infection.

first_line_drugs.notes: 200-300 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h for meningitis dosing; 3 weeks minimum
5 mg/kg IV q24h (term) or q36-48h (preterm); monitor troughs <2 mcg/mL
200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h; better CSF penetration than gentamicin

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q8-12h for suspected MRSA or late-onset; target trough 10-15
40 mg/kg IV q8h for resistant gram-negative meningitis

notes: Treat minimum 21 days or 14 days after CSF sterilization, whichever is longer. Repeat LP at 48-72h to document sterilization.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h (EOS); covers GBS and Listeria
5 mg/kg IV q24h (term >7d) or q36-48h (preterm); synergy with ampicillin

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q8-12h for late-onset or suspected MRSA; monitor troughs
50 mg/kg IV q8-12h for late-onset suspected gram-negative sepsis
6 mg/kg IV q72h (<30 wk) for fungal prophylaxis in ELBW; or 12 mg/kg for treatment

notes: EOS: ampicillin + gentamicin first-line. LOS: vancomycin + gentamicin or cefotaxime. Obtain blood culture before antibiotics; stop at 36-48h if culture negative.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV over 15-30 min; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total if responsive
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion for fluid-refractory shock
Empiric antibiotics within 1h of recognition; meningitis dosing if suspected

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV; alternative first-line vasopressor to epinephrine
1-2 mg/kg IV q8h for catecholamine-resistant shock; stress dose steroids
0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min IV for warm shock with low SVR

notes: Start vasopressors if still shocked after 40-60 mL/kg fluid. Monitor lactate, UOP, and perfusion. Hydrocortisone for refractory shock.
guideline_changes: Epinephrine now favored equally with dopamine as first-line vasopressor per recent RCTs

first_line_drugs.notes: 500 IU IM as single dose (2 separate sites); neutralizes unbound toxin
15 mg/kg IV load, then 7.5 mg/kg IV q12h x7-10 days; preferred over penicillin
0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV q1-4h PRN for spasms; or infusion 5-15 mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided q6h as adjunct for spasm control
50 mg/kg IV loading then 30-50 mg/kg/day infusion for autonomic dysfunction

notes: Neonatal tetanus is preventable with maternal TT vaccination. Monitor respiratory function closely; intubation may be needed for severe spasms.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h; covers streptococci and gram-positive organisms
5 mg/kg IV q24h; covers gram-negative organisms; synergy with ampicillin

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q8-12h if MRSA suspected; adjust based on culture results
5-7.5 mg/kg IV q8h if anaerobic or necrotizing fasciitis concern
Rarely needed; only for necrotizing fasciitis or abscess formation

notes: Obtain wound and blood cultures before antibiotics. Full sepsis workup if systemic signs present. S. aureus and E. coli are most common pathogens.

diagnosis_name: Disturbance of temperature regulation of newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: Servo-controlled to maintain axillary temp 36.5-37.4C; for hypothermia rewarming
Kangaroo mother care for stable infants; effective thermoregulation method
Apply at birth for preterms <32 wk without drying; reduces heat loss

second_line_drugs.notes: Warmed NS/D10W for severely hypothermic infants; slow rewarming 0.5C/h
Obtain cultures and start empiric antibiotics if hypothermia unexplained

notes: Hypothermia (<36.5C) increases mortality in neonates. Rewarm slowly at 0.5C/h. Unexplained temperature instability warrants sepsis workup.

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 10-20 mL/kg over 15-30 min for shock; repeat as needed up to 40-60 mL/kg
D10W + 0.45% NaCl at maintenance rate; adjust Na+ by <=10-12 mEq/L/day
Assess latch, frequency, and adequacy; supplement if weight loss >10%

second_line_drugs.notes: Small frequent breastmilk/formula feeds if mild and tolerated orally
Check Na, K, BUN, Cr q8-12h during correction; slow correction for hypernatremia

notes: Hypernatremic dehydration common in breastfed neonates with poor intake. Correct sodium slowly (<=10-12 mEq/L/day) to prevent cerebral edema.

first_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg (200 mg/kg) IV push for BG <25 or symptomatic; then infusion
D10W at GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min; increase by 2 mg/kg/min q30min to max 12-15
Breastfeed or formula within 1h of birth for at-risk infants; q2-3h

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL/kg buccal massage for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia; recheck in 30 min
0.02-0.03 mg/kg IM/IV (max 1 mg) for refractory hypoglycemia; temporary bridge
5-15 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h for persistent hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia

notes: Screen at-risk groups: IDM, SGA, LGA, preterm. Target BG >45 mg/dL after 4h of life. Max peripheral dextrose D12.5%; higher needs central line.
guideline_changes: Dextrose gel 40% increasingly adopted as first intervention for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia

first_line_drugs.notes: Intensive: irradiance >=30 mcW/cm2/nm; use hour-specific nomogram for thresholds
D10W + electrolytes at 1.5x maintenance if dehydrated or TSB near exchange level

second_line_drugs.notes: Double-volume 160 mL/kg; if TSB exceeds exchange threshold despite phototherapy
0.5-1 g/kg IV over 2h for isoimmune hemolytic disease approaching exchange level

notes: Use AAP hour-specific bilirubin nomogram with risk factors. Measure TSB q4-8h during phototherapy. Rebound check 12-24h after stopping.
guideline_changes: AAP 2022 raised phototherapy thresholds by ~2 mg/dL for healthy term infants

diagnosis_name: Transitory neonatal disorders of calcium and magnesium metabolism

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mL/kg (10-20 mg/kg elemental Ca) IV slow push over 10-30 min with cardiac monitoring
50-75 mg/kg/day elemental Ca IV continuous or 80 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg IV/IM q8-12h for hypomagnesemia; monitor for bradycardia
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/day PO for persistent hypocalcemia from hypoparathyroidism

notes: IV calcium must be given slowly under cardiac monitoring to prevent bradycardia. Correct hypomagnesemia first as it impairs Ca correction.

diagnosis_name: Neonatal withdrawal symptoms from maternal drug use

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.04-0.08 mg/kg PO q3-4h; titrate by 0.04 mg/kg per Finnegan score; wean 10-20%/day
Eat-Sleep-Console model: swaddling, low-stimulation, rooming-in, breastfeeding

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO q6h; longer acting; may reduce treatment duration
0.5-1 mcg/kg PO q4-6h as adjunct for refractory symptoms
Loading 20 mg/kg, maintenance 5 mg/kg/day; for polysubstance or non-opioid withdrawal

notes: Eat-Sleep-Console approach reduces pharmacotherapy use by 50%. Prioritize rooming-in and breastfeeding (if not contraindicated).
guideline_changes: Eat-Sleep-Console (ESC) approach replacing Finnegan scoring at many centers; reduces pharmacotherapy need

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 10-20 mL/kg IV over 15-30 min via UVC; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total
0.01-0.03 mg/kg IV (0.1-0.3 mL/kg of 1:10,000) via UVC; repeat q3-5 min
Effective PPV is the most critical step; initiate within 60 seconds of birth

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion for fluid-refractory hypotension
O-negative 10-20 mL/kg for hemorrhagic shock; uncrossmatched if emergent
1-2 mg/kg IV for vasopressor-resistant shock; adrenal insufficiency

notes: Identify shock etiology: hypovolemic, cardiogenic, or distributive. Effective ventilation is the single most important intervention in neonatal resuscitation.

first_line_drugs.notes: 21-30% FiO2 for term, increase to 100% if no response; initiate within 60 sec
3:1 ratio with PPV; 90 compressions + 30 breaths/min if HR <60 despite PPV
0.01-0.03 mg/kg IV (1:10,000) via UVC if HR <60 after 60s of CC + PPV

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg via ETT if IV access delayed; less reliable absorption
NS or O-neg blood 10 mL/kg IV over 5-10 min if hypovolemia suspected

notes: Effective ventilation is the single most important step. Evaluate for therapeutic hypothermia if moderate-severe encephalopathy develops.
guideline_changes: NRP 2025 updates: establish alternate airway before compressions; epinephrine after 60s of CC

first_line_drugs.notes: Monitor size, watch for jaundice; resolves spontaneously in 2 weeks to 3 months
Serial TSB if jaundice develops; phototherapy per AAP thresholds if indicated

second_line_drugs.notes: If hyperbilirubinemia develops from hematoma reabsorption; per hour-specific nomogram
Monitor hemoglobin if large cephalohematoma; transfuse only if symptomatic anemia

notes: Do NOT aspirate cephalohematoma — risk of infection. Resolves spontaneously. Calcified rim may persist for months but is benign.

first_line_drugs.notes: Cool to 33.5-34.5C within 6h of birth; maintain x72h then rewarm 0.5C/h
Correct acidosis, maintain euglycemia, avoid hyperthermia, seizure monitoring

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV loading for seizures; additional 10 mg/kg if seizures persist
1000 IU/kg IV q24h x5d; neuroprotective adjunct under study; not yet standard
Cooling at 6-24h may provide small benefit if missed <6h window

notes: Eligible: >=36 wk GA with moderate-severe HIE. Requires continuous neuromonitoring (aEEG) and MRI at 4-7 days. Mild HIE not cooled outside trials.
guideline_changes: AAP 2026 clinical report: hypothermia may benefit if started 6-24h post-birth; mild HIE still not indicated outside trials

diagnosis_name: Intracranial nontraumatic hemorrhage of fetus and newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: Maintain hemodynamic stability, correct coagulopathy, minimize handling
Head US at Day 3-7, repeat at 14d and 36 wk PMA; monitor for hydrocephalus

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg for symptomatic anemia from hemorrhage; maintain Hb >10 in acute phase
Neurosurgery consult for progressive post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus
25 mg/kg/day PO for temporizing slowly progressive hydrocephalus; limited evidence

notes: Prevention key: antenatal steroids, delayed cord clamping, minimal handling. Most grade I-II IVH resolve; grade III-IV carry higher neurodevelopmental risk.

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV loading over 10-15 min; additional 10 mg/kg if seizures persist (max 40)
Check and correct glucose, calcium, magnesium, sodium before/with anticonvulsants

second_line_drugs.notes: 40-60 mg/kg IV loading; maintenance 10 mg/kg IV q8h; fewer sedation side effects
20 mg/kg IV PE loading; second-line per ILAE if phenobarbital fails
0.05-0.15 mg/kg IV bolus then 0.1-0.4 mg/kg/h infusion for refractory seizures

notes: Phenobarbital is first-line regardless of etiology (ILAE 2023). Confirm seizures with aEEG/EEG when possible. Treat underlying cause concurrently.
guideline_changes: ILAE 2023 guidelines confirm phenobarbital as first-line; levetiracetam has lower efficacy (28% vs 80%)

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg IV q8-12h; covers GBS, Listeria, and common pathogens
5 mg/kg IV q24-48h based on GA; synergistic with ampicillin for gram-negatives
Supplemental O2, CPAP, or mechanical ventilation based on severity

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h; alternative to gentamicin if renal concerns or CSF coverage needed
Consider if concurrent RDS; poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via ETT

notes: Treat for 7-10 days; shorter courses (<=5 days) may suffice for culture-negative cases. Obtain blood culture before antibiotics.

first_line_drugs.notes: Supplemental O2, CPAP, or MV; PEEP 6-8 cmH2O; rate 40-60; Ti 0.5-0.6s
100-200 mg/kg via ETT for severe MAS with high FiO2 requirement; may reduce ECMO need
20 ppm for PPHN complicating MAS (OI >15-25); wean as oxygenation improves

second_line_drugs.notes: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation for refractory hypoxemia or air trapping
Ampicillin + gentamicin empirically; MAS mimics pneumonia and culture often sent
For severe refractory respiratory failure; last resort after maximal medical therapy

notes: Supportive care is the mainstay; no specific therapy. Do not routinely suction non-vigorous infants. Most resolve in 3-5 days.
guideline_changes: Routine endotracheal suctioning of non-vigorous meconium-stained infants no longer recommended (since 2015 NRP)

first_line_drugs.notes: Loading 20 mg/kg IV/PO, maintenance 5-10 mg/kg/day; may increase to 10 if persistent
Nasal CPAP 5-7 cmH2O to splint airway and reduce apnea frequency

second_line_drugs.notes: Nasal intermittent PPV; may be more effective than CPAP for severe apnea
0.5-2 mg/kg/h IV infusion; for caffeine-refractory apnea; limited use

notes: Caffeine citrate (CAP trial) reduces apnea, BPD, and improves neurodevelopment. Rule out sepsis, anemia, and metabolic causes for new-onset apnea.

first_line_drugs.notes: 23-25G needle or 18-24G angiocath; 2nd-3rd ICS MCL; for tension pneumothorax emergently
Pigtail catheter (6-8 Fr) or trocar; connect to underwater seal -10 to -20 cmH2O suction

second_line_drugs.notes: For small asymptomatic pneumothorax; high-flow or 100% O2 may hasten reabsorption
Transition to HFOV if pneumothorax recurs on conventional ventilation

notes: Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate needle decompression. Transillumination is rapid bedside diagnostic tool.

first_line_drugs.notes: Nasal CPAP 5-8 cmH2O from birth; first-line respiratory support for preterm
Titrate to SpO2 90-95% (preterm) or 92-97% (term); avoid hyperoxia
Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg if FiO2 >0.3 on CPAP for RDS; via LISA if available

second_line_drugs.notes: Intubate if FiO2 >0.5-0.6 on CPAP, recurrent apnea, or respiratory failure
1-8 L/min for mild-moderate respiratory distress; heated humidified

notes: Identify underlying cause: RDS, TTN, pneumonia, pneumothorax, or CDH. Chest X-ray and blood gas guide management. Most TTN resolves by 72h.

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 20 ppm when OI >15-25; wean by 5 ppm increments; avoid abrupt stop
Maintain preductal SpO2 >95%; target PaO2 60-80 mmHg; avoid hyperoxia
Optimize lung recruitment; target pH 7.35-7.45; avoid overventilation

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg PO q6h or 0.4 mg/kg IV over 3h; for iNO-refractory PPHN
0.33-0.99 mcg/kg/min IV; inodilator for PPHN with cardiac dysfunction
1-20 ng/kg/min IV; prostacyclin analog for refractory PPHN
For severe refractory PPHN (OI >40) failing maximal medical therapy

notes: iNO is the only FDA-approved pulmonary vasodilator for neonatal PPHN. 30% are iNO non-responders; sildenafil is best alternative.
guideline_changes: Sildenafil IV formulation and milrinone increasingly used for iNO-refractory PPHN

diagnosis_name: Pulmonary hemorrhage originating in perinatal period

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL of 1:10,000 via ETT for vasoconstriction; may repeat x1
Raise PEEP to 8-10 cmH2O or switch to HFOV to tamponade bleeding
10-15 mL/kg to replace blood loss; maintain Hb >12 during acute phase

second_line_drugs.notes: 100-200 mg/kg after bleeding stops; restores surfactant inactivated by blood/protein
10-15 mL/kg FFP for coagulopathy; vitamin K 1 mg IV if not already given
50-100 mcg/kg IV for refractory hemorrhage; case reports only

notes: Life-threatening emergency. Increase MAP to tamponade bleeding, correct coagulopathy, and transfuse aggressively. Surfactant after bleeding stops.

first_line_drugs.notes: Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via thin catheter on CPAP; within 2h of birth preferred
Start 6-8 cmH2O immediately after birth; primary respiratory support
Betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 to mother 24h apart before preterm delivery

second_line_drugs.notes: Intubate-Surfactant-Extubate technique if LISA not available
Volume-targeted; TV 4-6 mL/kg if intubation required; minimize duration
20 mg/kg loading then 5-10 mg/kg/day; facilitates extubation and prevents apnea

notes: LISA reduces need for mechanical ventilation and BPD compared to INSURE. Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg superior to lower doses and beractant.
guideline_changes: LISA (less invasive surfactant administration) recommended over INSURE where expertise available

first_line_drugs.notes: FiO2 to maintain SpO2 92-97%; usually <40% FiO2 sufficient
5-7 cmH2O nasal CPAP if moderate respiratory distress; aids fluid clearance
D10W + electrolytes at maintenance; NPO until RR <60-70 to reduce aspiration risk

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-8 L/min heated humidified; alternative to CPAP for mild TTN
OG/NG feeds if RR 60-80 and unable to safely PO feed

notes: TTN is self-limiting; resolves within 24-72h. No proven pharmacotherapy (diuretics, salbutamol, steroids not recommended). Rule out other causes.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID; anticholinergic for detrusor overactivity; max 5 mg TID
CIC 4-6x daily from birth; cornerstone of neurogenic bladder management
0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO BID; antimuscarinic alternative if oxybutynin not tolerated

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.4-0.8 mg/kg PO daily; beta-3 agonist for refractory detrusor overactivity
6-12 units/kg intradetrusor injection; max 200 units; for refractory cases
Surgical bladder augmentation for patients refractory to medical therapy

notes: Proactive CIC and antimuscarinics from early life preserve upper tract function. Urodynamic studies guide therapy; monitor renal function and bladder compliance regularly.
guideline_changes: Beta-3 agonists (mirabegron) now approved for pediatric use

first_line_drugs.notes: Timed voiding, bladder training, pelvic floor exercises; first-line for all pediatric bladder dysfunction
0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID; anticholinergic for overactive bladder; max 5 mg TID

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg PO daily (>5 yr); beta-3 agonist for refractory OAB
Intradetrusor injection 50-200 units for refractory detrusor overactivity
0.2-0.6 mg PO at bedtime for associated nocturnal enuresis; restrict fluids

notes: Behavioral modifications are first-line before pharmacotherapy. Screen for bladder-bowel dysfunction as combined management improves outcomes.
guideline_changes: FDA approval of beta-3 agonists for pediatric bladder dysfunction

diagnosis_name: Congenital obstructive defects of renal pelvis and congenital malformations of ureter

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial renal US q3-6mo for mild-moderate hydronephrosis; majority resolve spontaneously
Open or laparoscopic surgical repair of UPJ obstruction if worsening function or SFU grade 3-4

second_line_drugs.notes: Amoxicillin 10-15 mg/kg PO daily or TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily for high-grade HN
Robotic-assisted laparoscopic approach; emerging in older children with UPJ obstruction
Surgical correction for obstructive megaureter or ureteral malposition if symptomatic

notes: Many cases resolve spontaneously; surgery reserved for declining renal function or recurrent infections. Early diagnosis via prenatal US enables timely monitoring.
guideline_changes: Robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty gaining wider pediatric use

first_line_drugs.notes: Target BP <50th-75th percentile; ACEi (enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO) or ARB preferred
Serial renal US, eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio q6-12mo; Mayo Imaging Classification for risk

second_line_drugs.notes: V2-receptor antagonist; slows cyst growth in ADPKD; pediatric trials ongoing; adult dose 45-90 mg/d
Shown to slow total kidney volume growth in children with ADPKD; dose per age-based statin protocols
Emerging cyst-modifying agent; IMPEDE-PKD trial ongoing; not yet standard pediatric use

notes: BP control with RAS blockade is cornerstone of renoprotection. Novel disease-modifying agents (tolvaptan, metformin) are under active investigation in pediatric ADPKD.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2025 first guideline on ADPKD; tolvaptan and metformin trials ongoing in children

first_line_drugs.notes: Urethroplasty at 6-18 months of age; technique based on severity (TIP, Mathieu, or staged)
Topical DHT cream or IM testosterone 2 mg/kg q4wk x2-3 doses preop for small glans/proximal hypospadias

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-1000 IU IM 1-2x/wk for 5-6 wk preop; stimulates local testosterone for penile growth
Bupivacaine 0.25% 1 mL/kg caudal block periop for analgesia; reduces opioid need

notes: Optimal surgical age is 6-18 months. Long-term follow-up is essential to detect stricture, voiding dysfunction, or recurrent curvature.
guideline_changes: 2024 EAU/ESPU Part II update on hypospadias surgical outcomes and follow-up

first_line_drugs.notes: Cystoscopic incision of valves within 48h of birth once metabolically stable; gold standard
Urethral catheter or suprapubic catheter as initial decompression in unstable neonates

second_line_drugs.notes: Cutaneous vesicostomy if infant too small or unstable for primary ablation; temporary diversion
Oxybutynin 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID for secondary bladder dysfunction post-ablation
Clean intermittent catheterization for persistent bladder dysfunction post valve ablation

notes: Prenatal diagnosis enables early intervention. Multidisciplinary follow-up (urology + nephrology) is essential to monitor bladder and renal function long-term.
guideline_changes: Miniaturized endoscopes (<2mm) now enable valve ablation in neonates >2 kg

diagnosis_name: Renal agenesis and other reduction defects of kidney

first_line_drugs.notes: Yearly BP, urine albumin-creatinine ratio; serial renal US to confirm compensatory hypertrophy
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if hypertension or proteinuria develops; target BP <75th percentile

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO once daily if ACEi not tolerated; renoprotective
Moderate protein intake, adequate hydration, avoid nephrotoxins; no routine sports restriction per AAP

notes: Solitary kidney predisposes to hypertension and proteinuria over time. Ambulatory BP monitoring preferred; multidisciplinary follow-up for complex CAKUT.
guideline_changes: 2022 Italian consensus on congenital solitary kidney management published

first_line_drugs.notes: Surgical fixation by 6-12 months corrected age; scrotal or inguinal approach for palpable testes
For nonpalpable testes; EUA then laparoscopic exploration; may be staged (Fowler-Stephens)

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-1500 IU IM 2x/wk for 4 wk; limited role; not recommended by AUA for unilateral UDT
Nasal spray 1.2 mg/day for 4 wk; limited efficacy; not widely recommended

notes: Refer by 6 months if no spontaneous descent. Imaging is not recommended pre-referral; orchidopexy before 12 months optimizes fertility and reduces malignancy risk.
guideline_changes: AUA 2025 guideline update reinforces early orchidopexy by 12 months

first_line_drugs.notes: Warm water soaks 2-3x daily; gentle retraction and hygiene; supportive care for mild balanitis
Apply to foreskin BID for 4-6 wk; for associated phimosis in children >3 yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply topically BID for 7-14 days if candidal balanitis suspected
Apply topically TID for 5-7 days for suspected bacterial infection; avoid neosporin in children
Cephalexin 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7 days if refractory or strep-positive

notes: Most cases resolve with hygiene and topical therapy. Refer to urology if recurrent or refractory to exclude balanitis xerotica obliterans.

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to foreskin tip BID with gentle retraction for 4-8 wk; ~65% success rate
Apply to foreskin BID for 4-8 wk; alternative topical steroid with good efficacy

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply to foreskin BID for 4-6 wk; potent steroid for resistant physiologic phimosis
Dorsal slit or limited preputioplasty for failed steroid therapy; foreskin-sparing alternative
Definitive surgical option for pathologic phimosis, BXO, recurrent UTIs, or failed steroid therapy

notes: Topical steroids are first-line; treatment can be repeated if phimosis recurs. BXO is an absolute indication for circumcision at any age.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for edema and fluid overload; avoid high doses if oliguric
0.06-0.3 mg/kg/day PO once daily for hypertension; max 5-10 mg/day based on age
Restrict fluid to insensible losses + urine output during acute phase; low-salt diet

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg PO q6-8h for hypertensive urgency; short-acting for acute BP control
250-500 mg PO BID-TID for 10 days if active strep infection; eradication only, does not alter GN course
IV pulse 30 mg/kg (max 1g) x3 days for rapidly progressive/crescentic disease only

notes: APSGN is self-limiting; supportive care with diuretics and antihypertensives is mainstay. Immunosuppression reserved for crescentic disease on biopsy.
guideline_changes: Updated CDC clinical guidelines for post-streptococcal GN published

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) daily x4 wk, then 1.5 mg/kg (max 50 mg) alt-day x4 wk
Albumin 1 g/kg IV over 2-4h with furosemide 1 mg/kg IV for severe edema/anasarca

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO for 8-12 wk for frequent relapsers; cumulative max 168 mg/kg
4-6 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; for steroid-dependent or steroid-resistant NS; monitor trough levels
1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID (max 2 g/day); for SDNS/FRNS; continue >=12 months
375 mg/m2 IV x1-2 doses; for SDNS refractory to standard steroid-sparing agents

notes: 85-90% of children achieve remission with steroids. Steroid-sparing agents selected based on toxicity profile, adherence, and relapse pattern.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2025 first dedicated guideline for nephrotic syndrome in children; rituximab now recommended

first_line_drugs.notes: IV pulse 30 mg/kg/day (max 1 g) for 3 consecutive days; then oral pred 1-2 mg/kg/day taper
IV pulse 500-750 mg/m2 monthly x6 or PO 2 mg/kg/day x8-12 wk; for induction

second_line_drugs.notes: 375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 for ANCA-associated RPGN; non-inferior to cyclophosphamide
5-7 exchanges over 7-14 days for anti-GBM disease or severe ANCA vasculitis with DAH
1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID for maintenance after induction; alternative to azathioprine
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for maintenance therapy after induction remission achieved

notes: Urgent renal biopsy is essential to guide therapy. Aggressive induction immunosuppression has markedly improved outcomes; dialysis-dependent AKI predicts poor prognosis.
guideline_changes: Rituximab increasingly used in pediatric ANCA-associated RPGN

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial urinalysis q3-6mo; monitor BP, proteinuria, serum creatinine; reassurance if isolated
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if proteinuria >0.5 g/day develops; renoprotective

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO if ACEi not tolerated; for proteinuric hematuria
Adjunctive therapy for IgA nephropathy with proteinuria >1 g/day; per KDIGO GN guidelines

notes: Isolated microscopic hematuria without proteinuria has good prognosis. Refer to nephrology if proteinuria, hypertension, or family history of renal disease.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for edema and volume overload; monitor electrolytes
0.06-0.3 mg/kg/day PO once daily for hypertension; max 5-10 mg/day
Fluid and sodium restriction; monitor renal function, electrolytes, complement levels

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO for persistent proteinuria or hypertension; renoprotective
1-2 mg/kg/day PO if biopsy shows crescentic or proliferative GN requiring immunosuppression

notes: Establish specific diagnosis via serology and biopsy when possible. Treatment is primarily supportive unless biopsy reveals disease requiring immunosuppression.

first_line_drugs.notes: IV NS bolus then maintenance fluids; ketorolac 0.5 mg/kg IV (max 30 mg) for acute colic
Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy for stones <2 cm; preferred first-line for most pediatric stones

second_line_drugs.notes: Flexible URS with laser lithotripsy for ureteral or lower pole stones; holmium laser preferred
0.4 mg PO daily (off-label) for medical expulsive therapy of distal ureteral stones 5-10 mm
1-2 mEq/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hypocitraturia prevention; adjust per urine citrate
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for hypercalciuria prevention; monitor electrolytes

notes: Metabolic evaluation with stone analysis is mandatory in all pediatric stone formers. Tailor prevention to stone composition; dietary modifications are first-line prevention.
guideline_changes: AUA 2025 updated surgical stones guideline with pediatric-specific sections

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial renal US q3-6mo; ~50% resolve spontaneously; SFU grade 1-2 typically managed conservatively
Amoxicillin 10-15 mg/kg PO daily or TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily for high-grade HN to prevent UTI

second_line_drugs.notes: Open or laparoscopic repair for UPJ obstruction with declining function or SFU grade 3-4
Diuretic renography to assess drainage and differential renal function; guides surgical decision

notes: About half of hydronephrosis cases resolve spontaneously. Surgery indicated for febrile UTIs, deteriorating hydronephrosis, or declining renal function on renogram.

first_line_drugs.notes: TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily or nitrofurantoin 1-2 mg/kg PO daily for VUR grade III-V or post-UTI
Treat bladder-bowel dysfunction with timed voiding, constipation management before surgical intervention

second_line_drugs.notes: Deflux (dextranomer/HA) subureteral injection for VUR grade III-IV; 70-80% success per injection
Open or robotic-assisted surgical correction for high-grade VUR refractory to conservative management
0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID if associated overactive bladder; treat BBD concomitantly

notes: Risk-based approach: low-grade VUR often resolves spontaneously. Treat BBD before considering surgery; antibiotic prophylaxis for recurrent febrile UTIs.
guideline_changes: 2024 EAU/ESPU VUR chapter updated with risk-based management approach

first_line_drugs.notes: Assess volume status; restrict fluids to insensible losses + urine output if oliguric; avoid fluid overload
1-2 mg/kg IV bolus or 0.1-0.4 mg/kg/h infusion; convert oliguric to non-oliguric AKI if euvolemic
Calcium gluconate 100 mg/kg IV, insulin-glucose, kayexalate for hyperkalemia; bicarb for acidosis

second_line_drugs.notes: Continuous renal replacement therapy for fluid overload >10%, refractory electrolyte/acid-base derangement
Preferred RRT modality in small children; for AKI refractory to conservative measures
0.2 mg/kg IV once for tumor lysis syndrome-associated AKI; prevents uric acid nephropathy

notes: Identify and treat underlying cause promptly. Avoid nephrotoxins; adjust all renally-cleared drug doses; fluid overload >10% associated with poor outcomes.
guideline_changes: Pediatric-specific AKI biomarkers (NGAL, KIM-1) increasingly used for early detection

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO daily; ACEi for proteinuria and BP control; target BP <50th percentile
50 U/kg SC/IV 3x/wk for CKD anemia; target Hb 11-12 g/dL; supplement iron first
Phosphate binder; dose titrated to normalize serum phosphorus; give with meals

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05 mg/kg/day SC for CKD-related growth failure; per KDOQI; continue until target height or transplant
Vitamin D 1000-2000 IU/day PO; calcitriol 0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/day if secondary hyperparathyroidism
Non-calcium phosphate binder; 400-800 mg PO TID with meals if calcium-based binders insufficient
0.45 mcg/kg SC/IV q2-4wk; longer-acting ESA alternative to erythropoietin

notes: Multidisciplinary CKD clinic follow-up essential. Preterm and SGA infants at increased CKD risk; early RAS blockade for proteinuria slows progression.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2024 major update with dedicated pediatric chapter including growth and development focus

first_line_drugs.notes: Urgent workup: renal US, electrolytes, urinalysis, serology; classify as AKI vs CKD to guide therapy
1-2 mg/kg IV/PO for fluid overload; dose adjust based on renal function response
Correct hyperkalemia, acidosis, hyperphosphatemia; restrict potassium and phosphorus intake

second_line_drugs.notes: PD or HD if conservative measures fail; PD preferred in young children; preemptive transplant evaluation
0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if CKD with proteinuria/HTN confirmed; avoid in AKI with hemodynamic instability

notes: Priority is distinguishing AKI from CKD via renal US (kidney size) and history. Multidisciplinary team approach with nephrology, dietitian, and social work.

first_line_drugs.notes: Watch and wait for noncommunicating hydrocele in infants; most resolve by 12-24 months of age
Inguinal approach hydrocelectomy if persists >12-24 mo, communicating type, or symptomatic

second_line_drugs.notes: Laparoscopic extraperitoneal closure; safe and effective in children; shorter recovery
10 mg/kg PO q6h post-op for pain control; acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q6h alternative; avoid narcotics in infants

notes: Noncommunicating hydroceles in infants usually resolve spontaneously. Spermatoceles are benign, asymptomatic, and rarely require intervention in children.
guideline_changes: Laparoscopic hydrocele repair increasingly adopted as safe alternative

first_line_drugs.notes: Bed rest, scrotal elevation, ice packs, ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h or acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q6h
Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM x1 + doxycycline 2 mg/kg PO BID x10 days; for sexually active adolescents only

second_line_drugs.notes: 4-6 mg TMP/kg/day PO divided BID for 10-14 days if UTI-associated epididymitis with positive culture
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 10-14 days for culture-positive bacterial epididymitis

notes: In prepubertal boys, antibiotics reserved for those with pyuria or positive urine culture. Refer to urology to evaluate for anatomic abnormalities.
guideline_changes: Evidence supports withholding antibiotics in prepubertal boys without pyuria

first_line_drugs.notes: Scrotal exploration and detorsion ASAP; >90% salvage if within 6h; do not delay for imaging
Fix both testes to dartos fascia at time of surgery; prevents recurrence and contralateral torsion

second_line_drugs.notes: Outward rotation (open book) at bedside as temporizing measure; does not replace surgery
Removal of non-viable testis if necrotic on exploration; consider prosthesis discussion later

notes: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency; salvage rate drops rapidly after 6 hours. Clinical diagnosis should not be delayed by imaging when suspicion is high.

first_line_drugs.notes: Urinalysis with microscopy, urine Ca/Cr ratio, serum creatinine, C3/C4, renal US; repeat UA x3 to confirm
Reassurance and serial monitoring q3-6mo if isolated microscopic hematuria without proteinuria or HTN

second_line_drugs.notes: Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if concurrent proteinuria >0.5 g/day develops
Treat underlying UTI if identified; cephalexin 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7-10 days

notes: Most isolated hematuria is benign; confirm persistence with 3 samples 2 weeks apart. Refer to nephrology if proteinuria, HTN, decreased C3, or family history of renal disease.

first_line_drugs.notes: First morning urine protein/creatinine ratio; repeat x3 to exclude orthostatic proteinuria; renal function panel
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO for persistent proteinuria >0.5 g/day; uptitrate q4-8 wk to effect

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO once daily if ACEi not tolerated; antiproteinuric
Sodium restriction <2 g/day enhances ACEi/ARB antiproteinuric effect; dietary counseling

notes: Exclude orthostatic proteinuria (benign) with first morning sample. Persistent non-orthostatic proteinuria warrants nephrology referral and consideration of biopsy.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg IV/IM daily (max 2 g) for initial empiric therapy; switch to oral when afebrile x24-48h
8 mg/kg PO daily (max 400 mg) for outpatient oral therapy; 10-14 day total course
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 10-14 days; oral alternative for outpatient treatment

second_line_drugs.notes: Ampicillin 100 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h + gentamicin 5-7.5 mg/kg/day IV; for neonates/young infants
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 750 mg/dose); reserve for resistant organisms in older children

notes: Oral therapy is equivalent to IV for non-toxic children >2 months. Obtain urine culture before antibiotics; VCUG if febrile UTI in child <2 years.
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 Redbook supports shorter 5-10 day courses for uncomplicated cases

diagnosis_name: Tubulo-interstitial nephritis, not specified as acute or chronic

first_line_drugs.notes: Identify and discontinue causative drug (NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPIs); treat underlying infection if present
1 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 4-6 wk then gradual taper; for drug-induced or immune-mediated ATIN

second_line_drugs.notes: 600-1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID for steroid-refractory TIN; emerging evidence
BP control, fluid/electrolyte management; monitor renal function for recovery; low-salt diet

notes: Removing the offending agent is most important step; most cases recover spontaneously. Steroids may accelerate recovery but evidence from RCTs is limited.
guideline_changes: Role of mycophenolate mofetil emerging for steroid-refractory TIN

diagnosis_name: Urinary tract infection, site not specified

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7-10 days; first-line for uncomplicated lower UTI
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 7-10 days; alternative oral first-line
8 mg/kg PO daily (max 400 mg) for 7-10 days; once-daily dosing improves adherence

second_line_drugs.notes: 6-12 mg TMP/kg/day PO divided BID for 7-10 days; avoid if local resistance >20%
5-7 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 7 days; for lower UTI only; not for pyelonephritis
50-75 mg/kg IV/IM daily (max 2 g) for toxic-appearing children or infants <2 months

notes: Obtain urine culture before starting empiric antibiotics. Screen toilet-trained children for bladder-bowel dysfunction; shorter courses may suffice for afebrile UTI.
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 supports shorter antibiotic courses (5-10 days) for uncomplicated UTI

first_line_drugs.notes: Early intervention PT/OT/Speech starting from infancy; individualized goals
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for associated seizures; titrate over 2-4 wks
Ongoing developmental support; fine motor and adaptive skill training

second_line_drugs.notes: 3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for seizures in infants
Surgical placement if oral feeding unsafe; for severe cases with dysphagia

notes: No treatment reverses microcephaly; management is supportive with early intervention services. Seizure control and nutritional support are key priorities.
guideline_changes: CDC/AAP updated developmental milestone surveillance tools in 2022

first_line_drugs.notes: Individualized surgical approach based on malformation type; e.g. Chiari decompression with duraplasty
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for associated seizures
Early intervention PT/OT/Speech to maximize cognitive and motor function

second_line_drugs.notes: For associated hydrocephalus; programmable valve preferred
15-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for seizures; monitor LFTs

notes: Management is highly individualized based on specific malformation and severity. Multidisciplinary team including neurosurgery, neurology, genetics, and rehabilitation is essential.

first_line_drugs.notes: Multidisciplinary PT/OT/Speech therapy; federally mandated for ages 0-3 yrs
1-3 sessions/wk targeting expressive/receptive language goals
Fine motor, sensory integration, adaptive skills training; 1-3 sessions/wk

second_line_drugs.notes: 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO if iron-deficiency anemia contributing to delay
Levothyroxine 10-15 mcg/kg/day PO if hypothyroidism identified

notes: Earlier identification leads to better outcomes; screen at 9, 18, and 30 months per AAP. Treat underlying causes (metabolic, genetic, nutritional) while providing therapy.
source: AAP 2022, CDC Learn the Signs Act Early
guideline_changes: AAP/CDC updated milestone checklists in 2022 raising threshold from 50% to 75%

first_line_drugs.notes: Physical therapy with task-specific motor training; evidence-based per APTA CPG; 1-3x/wk
Activity-based intervention targeting daily living skills and fine motor tasks
Structured home exercises and environmental modifications per therapist guidance

second_line_drugs.notes: Cognitive Orientation to Occupational Performance; child-directed problem solving for motor tasks
0.3-1 mg/kg/day PO for comorbid ADHD if present; start low and titrate

notes: Screen for comorbid ADHD, ASD, and language disorders which are common. No pharmacotherapy for DCD itself; intervention focuses on task-oriented motor skill training.

diagnosis_name: Specific developmental disorders of speech and language

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured parent training (e.g. Heidelberg model) for expressive language delay; start by age 2-3
Direct child-centered therapy by SLP; 1-3 sessions/wk; phonological and language-focused
Targeted phonological or integrated phonological treatment for speech sound disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: Picture exchange, speech-generating devices for severe expressive impairment
Motor approach for isolated phonetic speech sound disorders

notes: Begin intervention by age 2-3 as only ~1/3 of late talkers catch up spontaneously. For multilingual children, provide therapy in all languages when possible.
guideline_changes: 2024 interdisciplinary CPG published with updated intervention recommendations for DLD

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO once at headache onset; max 400 mg/dose; 68% response rate in trials
15 mg/kg PO once at headache onset; max 1000 mg/dose; 54% response rate

second_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg PO if <40 kg, 10 mg PO if >=40 kg; for moderate-severe headache
5-20 mg intranasal once; FDA-approved for adolescents >=12 yrs

notes: Rule out secondary causes with red flag screening before treating as primary headache. NSAIDs are first-line; triptans reserved for moderate-severe or NSAID-refractory cases.
guideline_changes: AAN guideline reaffirmed Oct 2025; IHS global acute migraine recs published 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO at onset; max 400 mg; first-line acute treatment
5 mg PO if <40 kg, 10 mg if >=40 kg; FDA-approved for pediatric migraine age >=6
5-20 mg intranasal; FDA-approved >=12 yrs for acute migraine
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for prophylaxis; only FDA-approved preventive in children 12-17

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-1 mg/kg PO qHS for prophylaxis; max 1 mg/kg/day; limited evidence vs placebo
1-4 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for prophylaxis; monitor HR and BP
6.25-12.5 mg PO once; FDA-approved >=12 yrs for acute migraine
Combined cognitive behavioral therapy with amitriptyline shown beneficial in CHAMP trial

notes: Acute treatment should be given early at headache onset for best effect. Evidence for preventive medications is limited; CBT combined with medication may be superior to medication alone.
guideline_changes: AAN acute and preventive guidelines reaffirmed Oct 2025

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h; max 4 g/day; 10-14 days
15 mg/kg IV q6h; target trough 15-20 mcg/mL; add empirically for DRSP coverage
0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 2-4 days; give 10-20 min before or with first antibiotic dose

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q6h; max 12 g/day; alternative to ceftriaxone
40 mg/kg IV q8h; max 6 g/day; for resistant organisms or penicillin allergy

notes: Do not delay antibiotics for LP; start empiric therapy immediately if meningitis suspected. Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces hearing loss especially in H. influenzae meningitis.
guideline_changes: NICE updated meningitis guidelines March 2024 with revised empiric antibiotic recommendations

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 20 mg/kg IV q8h x 21 days; Children >3mo: 10 mg/kg IV q8h x 14-21 days
100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h empirically until bacterial meningitis excluded

second_line_drugs.notes: 20-30 mg/kg/day IV x 3-5 days for autoimmune/ADEM; max 1 g/day
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for autoimmune encephalitis or ADEM refractory to steroids
5 mg/kg IV q12h for CMV encephalitis in immunocompromised

notes: Start acyclovir empirically in all suspected encephalitis; do not wait for HSV PCR results. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR) increasingly recognized and requires immunotherapy.

diagnosis_name: Meningitis due to other and unspecified causes

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h; empiric until etiology clarified; max 4 g/day
15 mg/kg IV q6h empirically; de-escalate once organism identified
10 mg/kg IV q8h empirically if viral (HSV) etiology possible; pending PCR

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 2-4 days if bacterial etiology suspected
50 mg/kg IV q6h; add for Listeria coverage in neonates and immunocompromised

notes: Start empiric antibiotics plus acyclovir until CSF culture, PCR, and gram stain clarify etiology. Up to 92% of aseptic meningitis cases receive empirical antibiotics pending workup.
guideline_changes: NICE 2024 guidelines updated empiric treatment recommendations

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h for headache and fever; max 40 mg/kg/day
15 mg/kg PO q4-6h for analgesia/antipyresis; max 75 mg/kg/day
Maintenance fluids; monitor for SIADH; restrict if serum Na <135 mEq/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO q8h; max 4 mg/dose; for nausea/vomiting
10 mg/kg IV q8h if HSV-2 meningitis confirmed or immunocompromised patient

notes: Most cases are self-limited and resolve in 1-2 weeks with supportive care. Admit neonates, immunocompromised, or those with altered mental status or seizures for observation.
guideline_changes: WHO launched first-ever meningitis diagnosis/treatment/care guidelines April 2025

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate weekly to 0.75-2 mg/kg/day; max 40 mg/day <8 yrs
OnabotulinumtoxinA 10-15 U/kg per session for focal spasticity; max 340 U total per session
Ongoing PT/OT; goal-directed task-specific training; minimum 2-3 sessions/wk
0.12-0.8 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for generalized spasticity; short-term use preferred

second_line_drugs.notes: ITB pump: test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal; for severe generalized spasticity refractory to oral meds
0.05 mg/kg PO qHS; titrate to 0.05 mg/kg TID; max 0.1 mg/kg TID
0.5 mg/kg PO BID; titrate to max 3 mg/kg QID or 400 mg/day; monitor LFTs
Surgical option for children with spastic diplegia age 3-8 yrs with good selective motor control

guideline_changes: 2024 China neurorestorative therapy CPG published with updated rehabilitation recommendations

first_line_drugs.notes: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt with programmable valve; antibiotic-impregnated catheter recommended (Level I)
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy; preferred in obstructive hydrocephalus >6 months age

second_line_drugs.notes: ETV with choroid plexus cauterization; lower shunt dependency rates in selected patients
25-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; temporary CSF reduction; NOT recommended for post-hemorrhagic

notes: Antibiotic-impregnated shunt tubing reduces infection risk (Level I evidence). ETV success depends on age and etiology; higher failure rates in premature and very young infants.
guideline_changes: 2024 narrative review supports increasing ETV+CPC use over VP shunt in select patients

first_line_drugs.notes: OnabotulinumtoxinA 10-15 U/kg per session into affected limb muscles; repeat q3-6 months
CIMT: restrain unaffected limb 2-6 hrs/day for 2-8 wks to promote affected side use
Task-specific upper and lower limb rehabilitation; 3-5 sessions/wk during intensive programs

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate to 0.75-2 mg/kg/day for associated spasticity
Short leg cast changed weekly x 4-6 wks for ankle equinus contracture after BoNT-A
ITB pump for severe refractory spasticity; test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal

notes: BoNT-A combined with intensive PT or CIMT gives best functional outcomes for hemiplegic CP. Address both upper and lower limb involvement for optimal rehabilitation.

first_line_drugs.notes: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt; use antibiotic-impregnated catheter (Level I); programmable valve preferred
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy for obstructive hydrocephalus; lower infection/reoperation rates

second_line_drugs.notes: ETV with choroid plexus cauterization; reduces CSF production; emerging evidence in infants
25-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID as temporizing measure; max 2 g/day; monitor electrolytes
For post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus; removes intraventricular clots; may reduce shunt need (Level III)

guideline_changes: 2021 CNS systematic review update reinforced antibiotic-impregnated shunt use

first_line_drugs.notes: Balance and coordination training; gait rehab; 4-wk inpatient rehab improves ataxia scores
5 mg PO BID for episodic ataxia type 2; reduces attack frequency (Level B AAN)
50 mg PO BID for mixed/Friedreich/SCA ataxias; probably improves ataxia signs

second_line_drugs.notes: 150 mg PO daily for Friedreich ataxia age >=16 yrs; FDA-approved 2023; NRF2 activator
PO for Niemann-Pick type C; FDA-approved 2024; dose per weight-based schedule
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia; for refractory cases
5-10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for episodic ataxia type 2 if 4-AP not tolerated

notes: Treatment is etiology-specific; identify underlying cause before selecting therapy. New disease-modifying agents for Friedreich ataxia and NPC represent major recent advances.
guideline_changes: FDA approved omaveloxolone (Skyclarys) 2023 for Friedreich ataxia; levacetylleucine (Aqneursa) 2024 for NPC

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 1 mg/day PO; increase 1 mg q3-5 days to 2 mg TID; max 30 mg TID; 51% response rate
1-5 mg/kg/day levodopa PO divided TID; diagnostic trial for dopa-responsive dystonia first
Focal injection into dystonic muscles; dose varies by muscle group; repeat q3 months

second_line_drugs.notes: Start 5 mg/day PO; increase 5 mg q3-5 days; max 40-80 mg/day divided TID; 10% response rate
GPi DBS for severe generalized dystonia refractory to medical therapy; best for DYT1+
ITB pump for severe dystonia-spasticity; test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal
0.01-0.03 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; adjunctive for dystonia with myoclonus

notes: Always trial levodopa first to rule out dopa-responsive dystonia (Segawa disease). Anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl) are most effective first-line oral agents in children.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.02-0.05 mg/kg/dose IM/IV for acute dystonic reaction; then 1-2 mg PO BID maintenance
1.25 mg/kg IV/IM for acute EPS; then 5 mg/kg/day PO divided QID; max 300 mg/day
Start 1 mg PO daily; titrate by 1 mg q3-5 days; for chronic movement disorders

12.5 mg PO daily; titrate to 12.5 mg TID; for chorea/hyperkinetic disorders; monitor for depression
1-4 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for essential/action tremor

guideline_changes: VMAT2 inhibitors emerging for tardive dyskinesia; novel gene therapies under investigation

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.4 g/kg/day IV x 5 days (total 2 g/kg); preferred in children; can also give 1 g/kg/day x 2 days
Monitor respiratory function q4h; intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg; DVT prophylaxis; pain management

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-1.5 plasma volumes x 5-6 sessions over 10-14 days; for children >10-15 kg; requires central line
5-10 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for neuropathic pain; titrate to effect

notes: IVIG preferred over plasmapheresis in children due to easier administration and fewer side effects. Monitor for respiratory failure and autonomic instability closely; ICU admission for severe cases.

diagnosis_name: Myasthenia gravis and other myoneural disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg PO q4-6h; start 30 mg TID then titrate to max 120 mg QID based on symptoms
1-2 mg/kg/day PO; start low and increase slowly to minimize transient worsening; taper once stable

600 mg/m2 PO BID; max 2 g/day; steroid-sparing; onset 2-6 months
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for exacerbations or pre-thymectomy
Weight-based IV q2wk for AChR-Ab+ generalized MG refractory to standard therapy; FDA-approved peds

notes: Children with ocular MG may achieve spontaneous remission; start with pyridostigmine alone initially. Thymectomy should be considered for generalized MG in children; vaccinate against meningococcus before eculizumab.
guideline_changes: FDA approved eculizumab expanded indication for pediatric generalized MG

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg); taper over 6-12 months once response achieved
15 mg/m2/wk PO or SC (max 25 mg/wk); preferred steroid-sparing agent; give with folic acid

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 g/kg IV monthly for refractory disease or severe dysphagia; as adjunct to immunosuppression
375 mg/m2 IV x 4 weekly doses for treatment-resistant IIM
