first_line_drugs.notes: 0.75 mg/kg/day PO for Duchenne MD; reduces strength loss; delays cardiomyopathy; long-term use
0.9 mg/kg/day PO for DMD; FDA-approved; possibly less weight gain than prednisone
Regular stretching, strengthening, and cardiopulmonary rehab; prevent contractures; ongoing

second_line_drugs.notes: 40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for DMD with nonsense mutations; EMA-approved
30 mg/kg IV weekly for DMD amenable to exon 51 skipping; FDA-approved
ACE inhibitor (enalapril 0.1 mg/kg/day) or beta-blocker started by age 10 for cardiomyopathy prevention
Gene therapy for DMD age 4-5; single IV dose 1.33x10^14 vg/kg; FDA-approved 2023

notes: Corticosteroids remain the cornerstone of DMD treatment and should be initiated when motor function plateaus. Gene therapies are emerging; specific genetic testing guides exon-skipping and gene therapy eligibility.
source: AAN 2016 (DMD corticosteroids), French Soc Ped Neuro 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 French guidelines on corticosteroid compound choice and side effect management in DMD

diagnosis_name: Anoxic brain damage, not elsewhere classified

first_line_drugs.notes: Cool to 33.5-34.5°C within 6h of birth x 72h then rewarm over 6-12h; for mod-severe HIE >=36wk GA

Maintain normoglycemia, normocarbia, avoid hypotension; target MAP for gestational age

guideline_changes: AAP released updated clinical report on therapeutic hypothermia for neonatal HIE Feb 2026

first_line_drugs.notes: 15-25 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate up to max 100 mg/kg/day (max 2 g/day); monitor electrolytes

Surgical; for progressive visual loss refractory to medical therapy
Lumboperitoneal or VP shunt for refractory headache with visual loss despite medical therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Maintain airway, normoglycemia, normothermia; treat raised ICP; target MAP for age; GCS monitoring

10 mg/kg IV q8h empirically pending HSV PCR if infectious etiology suspected

2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for autoimmune encephalopathy refractory to steroids

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/day IV divided q12h; max 4 g/day; for aerobic gram-negative and streptococcal coverage

first_line_drugs.notes: 15-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; first-line for generalized epilepsy; monitor LFTs; avoid in pregnancy

8-10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; titrate to 30-46 mg/kg/day; for focal seizures

first_line_drugs.notes: MDI: 2-4 puffs via spacer q4-6h PRN; Neb: 0.15 mg/kg (min 2.5 mg) q4-6h PRN

first_line_drugs.notes: Titrate to SpO2 92-97%; via nasal cannula, face mask, or high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC)

Tidal volume 6-8 mL/kg IBW; PEEP per ARDSNet table; plateau pressure <28 cmH2O

first_line_drugs.notes: Tidal volume 6-8 mL/kg IBW; PEEP >=5 cmH2O per FiO2 table; Pplat <28 cmH2O

first_line_drugs.notes: Definitive removal under general anesthesia; procedure of choice in pediatric FB aspiration

Child >1 yr: subdiaphragmatic thrusts until object expelled or child becomes unresponsive

first_line_drugs.notes: Via nasal cannula if SpO2 persistently <90%; titrate to maintain SpO2 >=90%
Gentle superficial suctioning of nares PRN; avoid deep suctioning
If unable to maintain oral hydration; NS or D5-NS at maintenance rate

<5 kg: 50 mg IM x1; >=5 kg: 100 mg IM x1; before first RSV season; high-risk 2nd season: 200 mg

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort

second_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) if resistant organisms suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: 200-300 mg/kg/day (ampicillin) IV divided q6h (max 12 g amp/day); empiric first-line

60 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6 g/day) for polymicrobial or resistant infection

diagnosis_name: Lung abscess and necrosis of lung

first_line_drugs.notes: 30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day); covers S. aureus and anaerobes
50-100 mg/kg/day IV once daily (max 4 g); combined with clindamycin for broad coverage
200-300 mg/kg/day (ampicillin) IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day); alternative empiric

40-60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 4 g/day) if MRSA confirmed; trough 15-20
For large (>4 cm) peripheral abscess not responding to 7-10 days of antibiotics
10 mg/kg IV/PO q8h (max 600 mg/dose); for MRSA if vancomycin not tolerated

notes: Prolonged IV antibiotics (2-4 weeks) followed by oral step-down (total 4-6 weeks) is standard. Surgical drainage is rarely needed if antibiotics started early.

first_line_drugs.notes: 18-20G needle; send fluid for cell count, glucose, LDH, pH, Gram stain, culture
Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg/day IV daily + clindamycin 30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h
10-14 Fr pigtail catheter for complicated effusion (pH <7.0, glucose <40, LDH >1000, pus)

second_line_drugs.notes: 40,000 IU in 40 mL NS (>=10 kg) BID x3 days via chest tube; dwell 4h then drain
Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery for multiloculated effusion or fibrinolytic failure
Furosemide 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for transudative effusion (e.g., nephrotic, CHF)

notes: Determine if effusion is transudative or exudative using Light criteria to guide management. Parapneumonic effusion requires antibiotics and may need drainage if complicated.

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; first-line oral therapy
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized uncomplicated cases
200,000-250,000 U/kg/day IV divided q4-6h (max 24 MU/day) if penicillin-susceptible

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for penicillin-resistant strains (MIC >=2)
30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) for penicillin-allergic patients
>=5 yr: 8-10 mg/kg/day PO/IV daily (max 750 mg) for beta-lactam allergy
40-60 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h (max 4 g/day) add if meningitis suspected or highly resistant

notes: High-dose amoxicillin (90 mg/kg/day) overcomes intermediate penicillin resistance in S. pneumoniae. Ensure PCV13/15/20 vaccination is up to date.

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 7 days; covers beta-lactamase
50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for hospitalized patients

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 12 g/day) alternative to ceftriaxone
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for beta-lactam allergy
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h; only if beta-lactamase negative confirmed
8-10 mg/kg/day (TMP) PO divided BID for 7-10 days as oral step-down alternative

notes: 25-50% of H. influenzae produce beta-lactamase; do not use ampicillin empirically. Use amoxicillin-clavulanate or a cephalosporin for empiric coverage.

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; outpatient empiric first-line
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized children
O2 if SpO2 <92%; IV fluids if dehydrated; antipyretics (acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q4-6h)

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for under-immunized or severe illness
10 mg/kg PO day 1 then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for school-age if atypical suspected
<=15 kg: 30 mg BID; 15-23 kg: 45 mg; 23-40 kg: 60 mg; >40 kg: 75 mg BID x5d if influenza
30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) add if necrotizing/MRSA suspected

notes: Viral causes predominate in children <5 years; antibiotics are not always required. Obtain CXR and labs to help differentiate bacterial vs viral etiology.
guideline_changes: IDSA/PIDS 2026 updated empiric CAP therapy recommendations

first_line_drugs.notes: 100% O2 via NRB mask for small (<20%) stable primary spontaneous PTX; monitor q4-6h
14-16G needle at 4th ICS mid-axillary line (preferred in peds); for tension PTX or large PTX
10-14 Fr pigtail catheter; connect to water seal or -20 cmH2O suction; 4th-5th ICS MAL

second_line_drugs.notes: 16-18G over-needle catheter with 3-way stopcock; for stable patients with large 1st episode PSP
One-way flutter valve attached to pigtail catheter for ambulatory management of stable PTX
For recurrent PTX (>=2 episodes); bleb resection + mechanical or chemical pleurodesis

notes: Tension pneumothorax requires immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube placement. The 4th ICS at the anterior axillary line is the preferred site in pediatric patients.

first_line_drugs.notes: O2 if SpO2 <92%; IV fluids if dehydrated; antipyretics PRN; humidified air
<1 yr: 3 mg/kg PO BID; 1-12 yr: weight-based (30-75 mg BID); start within 48h x5 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-<12 yr, >=20 kg: weight-based single dose PO; >=12 yr & 40-80 kg: 40 mg x1; >80 kg: 80 mg
>=3.5 kg: 5 mg/kg IV day 1, then 2.5 mg/kg IV daily for 5 days; for hospitalized pts
Aerosolized: 6 g reconstituted in 300 mL via SPAG-2 x12-18 hr/day; severe immunocompromised RSV

notes: Most viral pneumonia in children is self-limiting and requires only supportive care. Oseltamivir should be started within 48 hours for confirmed or suspected influenza.

diagnosis_name: Respiratory tuberculosis, bacteriologically and histologically confirmed

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 300 mg); give with pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day
10-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 600 mg); take on empty stomach
30-40 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2 g); used in intensive phase (first 2 months)
15-25 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2.5 g); used in intensive phase; monitor visual acuity

second_line_drugs.notes: Isoniazid + rifapentine + pyrazinamide + moxifloxacin daily x4 months; for adolescents >=12 yr
15-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 750 mg); for drug-resistant TB or ethambutol intolerance
15-30 mg/kg/day IV/IM daily (max 1 g); for MDR-TB regimens; monitor audiology

notes: Standard regimen: 2 months HRZE then 2-4 months HR (4-month total for non-severe, 6-month for severe/cavitary). Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) is recommended.
guideline_changes: ATS/CDC/ERS/IDSA 2025 update: 4-month regimen (2HRZE/2HR) now standard for non-severe pediatric TB

diagnosis_name: Respiratory tuberculosis, not confirmed bacteriologically or histologically

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 300 mg); give with pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day
10-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 600 mg); take on empty stomach
30-40 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2 g); intensive phase (first 2 months)

second_line_drugs.notes: 15-25 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2.5 g); add to intensive phase if drug resistance suspected
Isoniazid + rifapentine + pyrazinamide + moxifloxacin daily x4 months; adolescents >=12 yr
15-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 750 mg); for drug-resistant TB or intolerance

notes: Clinically diagnosed TB (smear/culture negative) in children is treated identically to confirmed TB. The 4-month regimen is non-inferior for non-severe disease per SHINE trial.
guideline_changes: 4-month regimen (2HRZ(E)/2HR) validated for clinically diagnosed non-severe pediatric TB per SHINE trial

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM x1 dose (max 16 mg); onset 2-3h, lasts 24-48h; effective for all severity
L-epi 1:1000: 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) or racemic epi 2.25%: 0.05 mL/kg (max 0.5 mL) neb
Cool mist therapy; limited evidence but commonly used for comfort; avoid steam (burn risk)

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 2-3 days; if dexamethasone unavailable
2 mg neb x1 dose; for patients unable to take PO dexamethasone or with persistent symptoms
70:30 or 80:20 helium-oxygen mixture via face mask; for severe croup with stridor at rest

notes: A single dose of oral dexamethasone is effective for all croup severities including mild. Observe for 2-4 hours after nebulized epinephrine due to rebound potential.

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6 months; for fever/pain
1-2 drops/sprays per nostril PRN for nasal congestion; safe for all ages including neonates

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.5-10 mL PO at bedtime; age >1 yr only; as effective as dextromethorphan for cough
Bulb suction or NoseFrida for infants before feeds and sleep; after saline drops

notes: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for the common cold. Cough/cold medicines should be avoided in children <4 years per AAP Choosing Wisely recommendation.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg PO once daily (max 1000 mg) or 25 mg/kg BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days
250 mg PO BID-TID (<27 kg) or 500 mg PO BID (>=27 kg) for 10 days for confirmed GAS
<27 kg: 600,000 U IM x1; >=27 kg: 1.2 MU IM x1; for adherence concerns

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days; non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy
12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 6 mg/kg/day x4 days; for anaphylactic PCN allergy
7 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 300 mg/dose) for 10 days; for type 1 PCN allergy

notes: Rapid strep test or throat culture should confirm GAS before starting antibiotics. Treatment prevents rheumatic fever and reduces symptom duration by 1-2 days.

first_line_drugs.notes: 45-90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 2 g/dose) for 10-14 days; standard first-line
90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 2 g/dose) for 10-14d; severe or risk factors

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IM/IV x1 dose (max 1 g); for vomiting or unable to tolerate oral; then switch to PO
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 1.8 g/day) for penicillin-allergic patients
Fluticasone 1 spray/nostril daily as adjunct to antibiotics; age >=2 yr

notes: Diagnose bacterial sinusitis by clinical criteria (persistent >10d, worsening, or severe onset). Watchful waiting for 3 days may be appropriate for mild uncomplicated cases.

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg PO once daily (max 1000 mg) or 25 mg/kg BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days
250 mg PO BID-TID (<27 kg) or 500 mg PO BID (>=27 kg) for 10 days for confirmed GAS
<27 kg: 600,000 U IM x1; >=27 kg: 1.2 MU IM x1; for adherence concerns

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days; for non-anaphylactic PCN allergy
12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 6 mg/kg/day x4 days; for type 1 PCN allergy
7 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 300 mg/dose) for 10 days; penicillin allergy alternative
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q4-6h or ibuprofen 10 mg/kg q6-8h PRN for pain/fever

notes: Confirm GAS with rapid strep test or culture before prescribing antibiotics. Tonsillectomy is considered for recurrent GAS tonsillitis (>=7 episodes/yr).

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6 months for fever/pain
Saline drops (infants) or spray/rinse (older children) PRN for nasal congestion

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.5-10 mL PO at bedtime for nocturnal cough; age >1 yr only (botulism risk in infants)
Bedside humidifier at night for cough/congestion relief; keep device clean
0.6 mg/kg PO x1 (max 16 mg) if laryngotracheitis/croup component identified

notes: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral URI. Avoid OTC cough/cold medications in children <4 years per AAP recommendation due to lack of efficacy and safety concerns.

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.25 mg/kg/dose PO daily; 6-23mo: 2.5mg, 2-5yr: 5mg, >=6yr: 10mg daily
2-5yr: 5mg PO daily; >=6yr: 10mg PO daily
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for acute allergic reactions, max 60mg, short course 3-5 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 75-375mg SC q2-4wks based on IgE level and weight; age >=1yr for food allergy
6mo-5yr: 4mg PO daily; 6-14yr: 5mg PO daily; >=15yr: 10mg PO daily
2-11yr: 30mg PO BID; >=12yr: 60mg PO BID or 180mg daily

notes: Second-generation antihistamines are first-line; avoid first-gen due to sedation and CNS effects. Identify and eliminate allergen triggers before escalating pharmacotherapy.
guideline_changes: Omalizumab approved Feb 2024 for IgE-mediated food allergy age >=1yr; Neffy nasal epinephrine approved Aug 2024

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg IM anterolateral thigh (1:1000), max 0.5mg; repeat q5-15min PRN
20 mL/kg IV bolus, repeat PRN for hypotension; max 1L/dose
1-2 mg/kg IV/IM/PO q6h, max 50mg/dose; adjunct only, not substitute for epi
1-2 mg/kg IV, max 125mg; for biphasic reaction prevention

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous for refractory anaphylaxis; max 10 mcg/min
20-30 mcg/kg IV bolus, max 1mg; for beta-blocker-associated refractory hypotension
2mg intranasal single spray; for patients >=30kg as alternative to IM epi

notes: Epinephrine IM is the ONLY first-line treatment; delays increase mortality. Observe 4-6h minimum post-reaction for biphasic anaphylaxis risk.
guideline_changes: Neffy (epinephrine nasal spray) approved Aug 2024 for patients >=30kg

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.25 mg/kg/dose PO daily; can updose to 2-4x standard dose if uncontrolled
2-5yr: 5mg PO daily; >=6yr: 10mg daily; updose to 2x if needed
6-11yr: 30mg PO BID; >=12yr: 180mg PO daily; updose up to 4x if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-300mg SC q4wks for chronic spontaneous urticaria; age >=12yr
6mo-5yr: 4mg PO daily; 6-14yr: 5mg; add-on to antihistamine
3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; for refractory CSU, short-term use
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO short course 3-7 days for acute flares only

notes: Updose second-gen antihistamines up to 4x before adding biologics. Avoid first-generation antihistamines per international guidelines.
guideline_changes: 2024 global update recommends same algorithm for children as adults with weight-based dosing

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60mg) or IV methylprednisolone 30mg/kg (max 1g) x3 days
15-20 mg/m2/wk PO or SC (max 40mg/wk) with folic acid 1mg/day
2 g/kg IV divided over 2-5 days, monthly for moderate-severe disease

second_line_drugs.notes: 375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 or 750 mg/m2 (max 1g) x2 doses 2wks apart
600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID) for steroid-sparing
10 mg/kg IV q4wks (max 1g); for refractory JDM
5mg PO BID or weight-based; emerging for refractory cases

notes: Combine corticosteroids with MTX from onset to improve outcomes and reduce steroid dependence. Sun protection and physical therapy are essential adjuncts.
guideline_changes: JAK inhibitors emerging for refractory JDM; treat-to-target recommendations published 2025

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/m2/wk PO (preferred) or SC, max 25mg/wk; with folic acid 1mg/day
5-10 mg/kg/dose PO BID, max 500mg BID; for mild oligo-JIA
20mg SC q2wks (<30kg) or 40mg SC q2wks (>=30kg); first-line biologic
1 mg/kg intra-articular (max 40mg large joint, 20mg small); for oligoarthritis

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.8 mg/kg SC weekly (max 50mg) or 0.4 mg/kg SC 2x/wk
sJIA: 8-12 mg/kg IV q2wks (<30kg) or 8mg/kg q2wks (>=30kg)
10 mg/kg IV q4wks (max 1g) for polyarticular JIA
6 mg/kg IV load then 3 mg/kg q3days; for MAS in sJIA refractory to steroids

notes: Early DMARD/biologic initiation improves inactive disease rates significantly (83% if biologic started within 6mo). Screen all sJIA patients for lung disease.
guideline_changes: Draft 2025 ACR update: prefer oral MTX, earlier biologics, less NSAID/steroid reliance; emapalumab approved 2025 for MAS

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500mg BID) for symptomatic relief
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40mg/kg/day or 2400mg/day)
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO short course for moderate inflammation, taper over 2-4 wks

second_line_drugs.notes: 30-50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 2g/day); for reactive arthritis
10-15 mg/m2/wk PO/SC if persistent >3mo; with folic acid supplementation
1 mg/kg per joint (max 40mg large, 20mg small) for monoarticular disease

notes: Rule out septic arthritis before immunosuppressive therapy. Most reactive arthritis in children resolves within 3-12 months with NSAIDs alone.

diagnosis_name: Other systemic involvement of connective tissue

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40mg/kg/day); up to 1/3 of MCTD controlled with NSAIDs
5 mg/kg/day PO (max 400mg/day); for arthritis, skin, and constitutional symptoms
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for moderate disease; taper as tolerated over weeks

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/m2/wk PO/SC for arthritis or myositis component; with folic acid
600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID) for organ-threatening features
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 3mg/kg/day) for Raynaud phenomenon
1-3 mg/kg/day PO daily for steroid-sparing maintenance

notes: Treatment is tailored to the dominant clinical phenotype (lupus-like, scleroderma-like, myositis-like). Monitor for pulmonary hypertension and ILD as major complications.

first_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg/kg/day PO (max 400mg/day); recommended for ALL SLE patients long-term
0.5-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60mg); taper rapidly once controlled; minimize cumulative dose
600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID); preferred for lupus nephritis induction
10 mg/kg IV q4wks or 200mg SC weekly; age >=5yr; add-on to standard therapy

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mg/kg/day PO for maintenance after nephritis induction; check TPMT first
500-1000 mg/m2 IV monthly x6 for severe nephritis or CNS lupus
375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 or 750 mg/m2 x2 doses; for refractory disease
23.7mg PO BID (adult dose); approved for lupus nephritis; limited peds data

notes: Hydroxychloroquine is cornerstone therapy for all pediatric SLE. Monitor growth velocity and pubertal development given cumulative steroid exposure risk.
guideline_changes: ACR 2025: uniform HCQ use, limit glucocorticoid duration, early biologic/DMARD introduction

first_line_drugs.notes: FMF: <5yr 0.5mg/day, 5-10yr 1mg/day, >10yr 1.5mg/day PO; PFAPA: same dosing
PFAPA: 1-2 mg/kg PO single dose at flare onset; aborts episode rapidly
1-2 mg/kg/day SC (max 100mg); for colchicine-resistant FMF or CAPS; age >=8mo

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-4 mg/kg SC q4-8wks (max 300mg); for CAPS age >=2yr and refractory FMF
Loading: 4.4 mg/kg SC then 2.2 mg/kg SC weekly (max 320mg); CAPS age >=2yr
Surgical; definitive for PFAPA if medical therapy fails; high cure rate

notes: Colchicine is lifelong for FMF to prevent amyloidosis. IL-1 blockade is highly effective for CAPS and colchicine-resistant autoinflammatory syndromes.

first_line_drugs.notes: Definitive cure; best outcomes if <3.5mo age; HLA-matched sibling preferred; ~97% survival
400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks; maintain trough IgG >500mg/dL; bridge to HSCT
5 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID for PJP prophylaxis until immune reconstitution

second_line_drugs.notes: Autologous CD34+ cell modification; for ADA-SCID and SCID-X1 without matched donor
3-6 mg/kg/day PO daily for fungal prophylaxis pre-transplant
20 mg/kg/dose IV q8h or 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID for HSV/VZV prophylaxis

notes: SCID is a pediatric emergency; early HSCT before 3.5 months yields best survival. Avoid live vaccines and use irradiated, CMV-negative blood products.
guideline_changes: Gene therapy trials showing >90% survival for SCID-X1 and ADA-SCID

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks; target trough IgG >500mg/dL (>700 if lung disease)
100-200 mg/kg SC weekly; equivalent efficacy to IVIG; fewer systemic reactions
20 mg/kg/day PO daily for breakthrough sinopulmonary infections if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO weekly (max 500mg) for recurrent respiratory infections
Enteric-coated 3-9mg PO daily for CVID-associated enteropathy
375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 for CVID-associated autoimmune cytopenias

notes: Lifelong immunoglobulin replacement is standard of care. Monitor for granulomatous disease, autoimmune cytopenias, and lymphoid malignancy.
guideline_changes: 2024 review supports SCIG as preferred over IVIG for replacement therapy in stable patients

diagnosis_name: Immunodeficiency associated with other major defects

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks for antibody deficiency component; target trough >500mg/dL
Definitive cure for WAS and severe DiGeorge; HLA-matched donor preferred; early referral
5 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID for PJP and bacterial infection prophylaxis

second_line_drugs.notes: For complete DiGeorge (T-cell absent); cultured thymus tissue allograft
Lentiviral CD34+ cell transduction for WAS without matched donor; investigational
For WAS with severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding; irradiated products only
Weight-based dosing PO daily for WAS thrombocytopenia; off-label in children

notes: Syndrome-specific management is essential; DiGeorge requires cardiac and endocrine care alongside immunology. WAS patients should receive early curative therapy.
guideline_changes: Gene therapy for Wiskott-Aldrich showing promising long-term outcomes in trials

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks; individualize trough target based on infection frequency
100-200 mg/kg SC weekly; preferred for home therapy; fewer systemic side effects
20 mg/kg/day PO for sinopulmonary infections pending Ig replacement initiation

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO weekly (max 500mg) for recurrent lower respiratory infections
HyQvia: rHuPH20 + IgG 10% SC monthly; allows higher volumes per site
Airway clearance techniques for established bronchiectasis; non-pharmacologic

notes: Antibody deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency group. Regular monitoring of lung function and CT chest for bronchiectasis is essential.
guideline_changes: 2024 review supports individualized trough targets rather than fixed 500mg/dL threshold

first_line_drugs.notes: 400-600 mg/kg IV q3-4wks if hypogammaglobulinemia confirmed; monitor trough levels
5 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID for patients at risk of opportunistic infections
Discontinue/reduce offending drug if secondary; nutritional repletion if malnourished

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO weekly (max 500mg) for recurrent sinopulmonary infections
3-6 mg/kg/day PO daily for fungal prophylaxis in combined defects
5-10 mcg/kg/day SC for congenital or severe neutropenia component

notes: Identify and treat reversible secondary causes first. Monitor immune function q6-12mo and initiate IgG replacement if recurrent infections persist.

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40mg/kg/day or 2400mg/day); age >=3mo
15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h (max 75mg/kg/day or 4g/day); if NSAIDs contraindicated
5-7 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500mg BID); for older children >=2yr

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 150mg/day); age >=1yr
Stretching, gentle strengthening, heat/ice; non-pharmacologic first-line adjunct

notes: Investigate underlying cause before chronic NSAID use; limit OTC NSAID to 10 days without physician review. NSAIDs reduce opioid requirements significantly in pediatric pain.

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60mg) x4-6wks then slow taper; or IV pulse 30mg/kg (max 1g) x3d
15-20 mg/m2/wk SC (max 40mg/wk) with folic acid 1mg/day; start at disease onset
2 g/kg IV divided over 2-5 days monthly for moderate-severe or refractory disease

second_line_drugs.notes: 600 mg/m2/dose PO BID (max 1.5g BID) as steroid-sparing agent
375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4; for refractory myositis with autoantibodies
1-3 mg/kg/day PO daily for maintenance; check TPMT before starting
5mg PO BID (or weight-based); emerging for refractory inflammatory myopathy

notes: Combine steroids with methotrexate from onset to achieve steroid-free remission. Steroid taper is slow (1-2 years) to prevent relapse; monitor CK and muscle strength.
guideline_changes: JAK inhibitors emerging as option for refractory inflammatory myositis

first_line_drugs.notes: Stool softeners, high-fiber diet, sitz baths after each BM; topical glyceryl trinitrate 0.2% ointment BID x6-8 wks
Simple fistulotomy for persistent fistula-in-ano; sphincter-sparing in children; heals by secondary intention
For perianal abscess: bedside or OR I&D under GA; pack wound loosely; sitz baths post-op

second_line_drugs.notes: Applied BID to anal margin for chronic fissure unresponsive to GTN; fewer headaches than GTN
Injection into internal anal sphincter for refractory chronic fissure; used off-label in older children
Complete excision of fistula tract for complex or recurrent fistulae; rarely needed in children

notes: Most pediatric perianal fistulae are superficial and may resolve spontaneously without sphincter involvement. Exclude Crohn disease in any child with recurrent or complex perianal disease.
guideline_changes: 2025 systematic review supports initial surgical intervention for recurrent pediatric perianal fistula

first_line_drugs.notes: Screen all neonates for congenital heart defects (AVSD in ~40%); surgical repair as indicated
TSH at birth, 6 mo, 12 mo, then annually; treat hypothyroidism with levothyroxine 10-15 mcg/kg/day
PT/OT/speech from infancy; developmental assessment by 12 mo; IEP by age 3
Eye exam by 6 mo then annually; audiology at birth, 6 mo, annually; ABR if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: Cervical spine X-ray if symptomatic; flexion/extension views before sports participation
Polysomnography by age 4 for obstructive sleep apnea; adenotonsillectomy or CPAP if positive
Tissue transglutaminase IgA at age 2 then as indicated; prevalence 5-16% in DS
Screen for ASD, ADHD, anxiety, depression; psychiatric meds used cautiously per AAP 2022 guidance

notes: Comprehensive health supervision requires lifelong multisystem screening per AAP schedule. The 2024 collaboration ensures guideline translations are freely available worldwide.
guideline_changes: 2022 AAP update added autism screening (7-19% prevalence), expanded psychiatric medication guidance

first_line_drugs.notes: Full resuscitation per NRP; thermoregulation, respiratory support, NG/G-tube feeding as needed
Echo at birth; diuretics + digoxin for CHF; palliative or corrective cardiac surgery if appropriate
NG tube or gastrostomy for poor feeding; treat GER medically first, fundoplication if refractory

second_line_drugs.notes: Comfort-focused care with family-centered decision-making when prognosis is very poor
Standard antibiotics for pneumonia, UTI, otitis media; these are leading causes of morbidity
Karyotype confirmation; recurrence risk counseling; prenatal diagnosis options for future pregnancies

notes: Median survival is 5-15 days for trisomy 13 and 5-15 days for trisomy 18, but long-term survivors exist with active intervention. Shared decision-making with families is essential.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 clinical report formally supports active management including resuscitation; no longer comfort-only default

diagnosis_name: Klinefelter syndrome and other male chromosome abnormalities

first_line_drugs.notes: Begin at puberty (age 11-14) if levels low; IM testosterone enanthate 50mg monthly, titrate upward
Speech-language therapy, educational support; screen for ADHD and learning disabilities by school age
Annual LH/FSH/testosterone from age 10; bone density (DXA) at puberty onset and periodically

second_line_drugs.notes: Alternative to IM injections; daily application; titrate to maintain physiologic levels
Micro-TESE for sperm retrieval in adolescents/young adults; counsel early about fertility options
CBT or counseling for anxiety, depression, social difficulties; multidisciplinary team approach
Reduction mammoplasty if persistent gynecomastia causes distress; typically after puberty

notes: Early diagnosis (ideally prenatal or infancy) allows proactive developmental and endocrine management. Multidisciplinary care including endocrinology, psychology, and andrology is essential.
source: European Academy of Andrology/ESE 2021, JCEM 2025 Update
guideline_changes: 2025 JCEM review expanded psychological and quality-of-life management recommendations

diagnosis_name: Other chromosome abnormalities, not elsewhere classified

first_line_drugs.notes: Chromosomal microarray + karyotype; consider exome/genome sequencing if CMA non-diagnostic
Early intervention referral; PT/OT/speech as indicated; developmental pediatrician follow-up
Cardiac echo, renal US, ophthalmology, audiology based on phenotype; tailor to specific abnormality

second_line_drugs.notes: Recurrence risk assessment; family karyotyping; prenatal diagnosis options for future pregnancies
Screen for ASD, ADHD, anxiety; therapy and medication as indicated by symptoms
Serial growth parameters; consider GH evaluation if short stature; endocrine referral as needed

notes: Management is symptom-directed as phenotypes vary widely by specific chromosomal abnormality. Regular multidisciplinary follow-up with tailored screening protocols is essential.
guideline_changes: Chromosomal microarray and exome sequencing now standard first-tier diagnostic tests

diagnosis_name: Other sex chromosome abnormalities, female phenotype, not elsewhere classified

first_line_drugs.notes: Full karyotype; CMA for mosaicism detection; includes Triple X (47,XXX) and other variants
Early intervention for speech/language delays; educational accommodations; IEP as needed
Puberty assessment; FSH/LH/estradiol if delayed puberty; bone age annually from age 8

second_line_drugs.notes: Low-dose estradiol if ovarian insufficiency or delayed puberty; start age 11-12, titrate gradually
Screen for anxiety, social difficulties, ADHD; CBT or behavioral therapy as needed
Assess ovarian function; discuss fertility preservation options; refer to reproductive endocrinology

notes: Triple X syndrome (47,XXX) is the most common condition in this category with generally mild phenotype. Individualized screening and support based on specific karyotype findings is key.
guideline_changes: Expanded use of chromosomal microarray for precise characterization of sex chromosome variants

diagnosis_name: Other trisomies and partial trisomies of autosomes

first_line_drugs.notes: Full resuscitation; respiratory support; NG/G-tube feeding; thermoregulation per NRP guidelines
Echo at birth; CHD present in majority; medical management of CHF; surgical repair when appropriate
Karyotype + CMA to define exact trisomy/partial trisomy; guides prognosis and surveillance plan

second_line_drugs.notes: Family-centered palliative approach when prognosis is very poor; shared decision-making essential
Early intervention PT/OT/speech; special education referral; developmental pediatrician follow-up
Prompt treatment of respiratory and urinary infections; influenza and pneumococcal vaccines on schedule

notes: Prognosis varies widely depending on specific chromosomal abnormality and degree of mosaicism. Shared decision-making with families regarding intensity of intervention is critical.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 guidance supports active management for trisomies previously considered lethal

first_line_drugs.notes: Somatropin 0.045-0.050 mg/kg/day SC; start from age 2 if growth failure; continue until bone age >=14
Low-dose transdermal estradiol at age 11-12; titrate over 2-3 yrs; add progesterone for cycling
Echo + cardiac MRI at diagnosis; aortic monitoring q3-5 yrs; screen for bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation
Renal US at diagnosis; TSH annually; autoimmune thyroiditis in ~25%; levothyroxine if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: Low-dose oxandrolone 0.03-0.05 mg/kg/day as GH adjunct for severe short stature; monitor virilization
Oocyte/ovarian tissue cryopreservation if ovarian function present; counsel in adolescence
Audiometry every 1-3 yrs; sensorineural and conductive loss common; ENT referral as needed
Screen for nonverbal learning difficulties, social anxiety; neuropsych evaluation recommended

notes: The 2024 international consensus is the most comprehensive TS guideline to date with 87 recommendations. Cardiovascular surveillance is the leading cause of excess mortality and requires lifelong monitoring.
source: International TS Consensus 2024 (Eur J Endocrinol), PES/ESE
guideline_changes: 2024 Aarhus guidelines updated GH dosing, cardiovascular surveillance, and fertility preservation protocols

first_line_drugs.notes: Millard or Fisher technique at 3-6 months; rule of 10s (10 wks, 10 lb, Hb 10g/dL); under GA
Pre-surgical orthodontic device from 1-2 wks of age; narrows cleft, molds nose; improves surgical result
Specialized bottles (Haberman, Dr. Brown's); lactation consultant; upright feeding position

second_line_drugs.notes: Preliminary procedure at 2-4 wks for wide clefts to approximate lip segments before definitive repair
Nasal revision at age 4-6 or after skeletal maturity; addresses residual nasal asymmetry
Scar revision and vermilion adjustment if needed; typically deferred until school age or later

notes: Multidisciplinary cleft team management from birth through adulthood is the standard of care. NAM if started within first 2 weeks significantly improves primary surgical outcomes.
source: ACPA 2024 Parameters of Care, WHO Smile Train Guidelines
guideline_changes: 2024 ACPA update emphasizes nasoalveolar molding (NAM) pre-surgical orthodontics

first_line_drugs.notes: Primary palate repair at 9-12 months; Furlow or von Langenbeck technique; before speech development
Palatal obturator if needed; specialized bottles; upright positioning; dietitian monitoring growth
PE tubes at time of palate repair or earlier for chronic OME; audiometry follow-up q6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: Begin by age 12-18 mo post-repair; target velopharyngeal competence; nasometry assessment at age 3-4
Secondary surgery for velopharyngeal insufficiency if persistent after age 4; improves speech
Mixed dentition stage (age 7-11); iliac crest bone graft to alveolar cleft for canine eruption
For symptomatic oronasal fistulae post-palatoplasty; local flaps or obturator

notes: Timing of palate repair balances speech outcomes (earlier is better) against maxillary growth (later preserves growth). Interdisciplinary cleft team follow-up is required through adolescence.
source: ACPA 2024 Parameters of Care, NEJM 2023 Timing Study
guideline_changes: NEJM 2023 RCT showed 6-month palate repair noninferior to 12-month repair for speech outcomes

first_line_drugs.notes: Lip repair at 3-6 mo, palate repair at 9-12 mo; NAM from birth if available; PE tubes at palatoplasty
Custom appliance from 1-2 wks; molds cleft lip, alveolus, and nose pre-surgically; improves symmetry
Specialized bottles; upright positioning; weight monitoring; supplemental NG feeds if growth faltering
ABR at birth; tympanometry regularly; PE tubes for chronic OME; hearing aids if sensorineural loss

second_line_drugs.notes: At mixed dentition (age 7-11); iliac crest autograft; enables canine eruption and stabilizes arch
Pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty for VPI; after age 4 if speech therapy insufficient
Le Fort I advancement at skeletal maturity (age 16-18) for midface hypoplasia; orthodontics pre/post
Final nasal reconstruction after skeletal maturity; septorhinoplasty for residual deformity

notes: Combined cleft lip and palate requires the most complex staged surgical protocol with 5+ procedures over 18 years. Multidisciplinary team follow-up from birth through adulthood is mandatory.
source: ACPA 2024 Parameters of Care, WHO/Smile Train Protocol
guideline_changes: 2024 ACPA emphasizes integrated NAM + staged surgical protocol; earlier palate repair gaining evidence

diagnosis_name: Congenital malformations of skull and face bones

first_line_drugs.notes: Open surgery at 6-12 months for single-suture craniosynostosis; fronto-orbital advancement for coronal
Minimally invasive option for infants <3-4 months; followed by helmet therapy for 6-12 months
IV TXA 10-15 mg/kg bolus then infusion intra-op; reduces blood loss during cranial vault surgery

second_line_drugs.notes: Post-endoscopic or for positional plagiocephaly; custom molding helmet worn 23 hrs/day for 6-12 mo
Le Fort III or monobloc distraction for syndromic craniosynostosis (Apert, Crouzon); age 4-8
For suspected raised ICP; papilledema screening; VP shunt if hydrocephalus develops
Mandibular or midface distraction for severe micrognathia/midface hypoplasia; external devices

notes: Best surgical outcomes for single-suture craniosynostosis occur before 6 months of age. Syndromic cases (Apert, Crouzon, Pfeiffer) require lifelong multidisciplinary craniofacial team care.
guideline_changes: Minimally invasive endoscopic suturectomy gaining wider adoption for early-presenting cases

first_line_drugs.notes: High ligation of processus vaginalis; day surgery; inguinal incision; gold standard technique
Equivalent outcomes to open; allows contralateral ring evaluation; shorter bilateral OR time
Incarcerated hernia: attempt gentle reduction then semi-urgent repair within 24-48 hrs

second_line_drugs.notes: Case-by-case decision; laparoscopic visualization of contralateral ring preferred over blind exploration
Sedation + Trendelenburg for incarcerated hernia; schedule repair within 2-5 days after reduction

notes: Pediatric inguinal hernias do not close spontaneously and always require surgical repair. Premature infants have higher incidence and should be repaired before NICU discharge or shortly after.
guideline_changes: AAP 2023 guideline: laparoscopic approach equivalent to open; allows contralateral ring evaluation

first_line_drugs.notes: Observe until age 4-5 yrs; 95% of defects <1cm close spontaneously; no strapping or coins
Suture repair (Mayo technique) if persistent after age 5 or symptomatic; day surgery; infraumbilical incision

second_line_drugs.notes: Immediate repair for strangulated hernia; resuscitate, broad-spectrum antibiotics, emergent OR
May consider repair before age 5 for large defects >2cm, proboscoid hernias, or symptomatic cases

notes: Umbilical hernias are extremely common in infants, especially in African American and preterm infants. Avoid referral to surgery before age 4-5 unless symptomatic or incarcerated.
source: AAFP/AAP Choosing Wisely, ACS Pediatric Surgery Guidelines
guideline_changes: Choosing Wisely recommends deferring surgical referral until age 4-5 years for asymptomatic cases

first_line_drugs.notes: Weekly long-leg casts from birth; correct cavus, adductus, varus, then equinus; typically 5-7 casts
Performed after casting if equinus persists (>85% of cases); outpatient under local anesthesia
Boots-and-bar orthosis 23 hrs/day x3 mo, then nighttime until age 5; critical for preventing relapse

second_line_drugs.notes: Daily stretching, taping, continuous passive motion; alternative to casting in select centers
For relapsed clubfoot with dynamic supination; transfer to lateral cuneiform; after age 2.5-3
Extensive surgical release reserved for severe resistant cases; higher complication rate than Ponseti
Gentle serial stretching and reverse-last shoes for non-clubfoot forefoot adduction; resolves in most

notes: The Ponseti method is the worldwide standard for clubfoot with 95% success rate when bracing compliance is maintained. Bracing noncompliance is the leading cause of recurrence.
guideline_changes: 2022 AAP report reinforces Ponseti method as definitive first-line with 95% success rate

first_line_drugs.notes: First-line for DDH diagnosed 0-6 months; 95% success in dysplasia/subluxation, 80% in dislocation
Graf method US at 4-6 wks for risk factors or clinical concern; universal screening under debate
Under GA with arthrogram for failed Pavlik or late diagnosis (6-18 mo); hip spica 12 wks

second_line_drugs.notes: For failed closed reduction or age >18 mo; medial or anterior approach; followed by spica cast
Salter innominate or femoral shortening osteotomy for residual dysplasia in older children
Rhino or Tubingen splint alternative to Pavlik for mild dysplasia; ensures hip in flexion/abduction

notes: Early detection and treatment is critical: Pavlik harness before 6 months yields best outcomes. Avascular necrosis of the femoral head is the most serious complication of treatment.
source: AAP 2024, AAOS DDH Quality Measure, BSCOS 2023 Consensus
guideline_changes: 2024 evidence increasingly supports universal ultrasound screening over selective screening

diagnosis_name: Congenital musculoskeletal deformities of skull, face, spine and chest

first_line_drugs.notes: Manual stretching, positioning, tummy time from birth; 2024 APTA CPG: start PT within 1-3 months for best outcome
Home stretching program, environmental modifications, positional strategies to prevent plagiocephaly
Custom cranial orthosis for positional plagiocephaly; start 4-6 mo, wear 23 hrs/day for 3-6 months

second_line_drugs.notes: OnabotulinumtoxinA into SCM for refractory CMT; used as adjunct to stretching in older infants
Unipolar or bipolar SCM release for persistent torticollis after >1 yr of PT; improves ROM
TLSO brace for congenital scoliosis monitoring; vacuum bell or Nuss bar for pectus excavatum
Hemivertebra excision or growing rods for progressive congenital scoliosis; individualized timing

notes: Congenital muscular torticollis resolves in >90% with early PT initiated before age 3 months. Congenital vertebral anomalies require serial imaging to monitor curve progression.
guideline_changes: 2024 APTA published first evidence-based CPG for congenital muscular torticollis physical therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg/kg/day x3 days q2-4 mo; start in infancy for moderate-severe OI; improves BMD, reduces pain
0.025-0.05 mg/kg q6mo; alternative to pamidronate; fewer infusion days; monitor calcium post-infusion
Gentle handling; lightweight splints/casts; minimize immobilization to prevent further bone loss
Low-impact PT/OT to build muscle strength and mobility; aquatic therapy; avoid contact sports

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg/kg/wk oral; less effective than IV bisphosphonates but avoids infusions; for milder types
Fassier-Duval rods for recurrent long bone fractures; grow with child; reduce fracture frequency
Anti-RANKL antibody; emerging use in children unresponsive to bisphosphonates; rebound risk on stopping
Screen for dentinogenesis imperfecta; preventive dental care; crowns for fragile teeth

notes: Start bisphosphonate therapy as early as possible since effect on skeleton is greatest during growth. Multidisciplinary care including orthopedics, endocrine, PT, and genetics is essential.
source: OIF Guidelines, Calcified Tissue International 2024 Review
guideline_changes: 2024 meta-analysis confirms IV bisphosphonates improve BMD; comparative trials still needed for optimal regimen

first_line_drugs.notes: Optimal age 6 mo-3 yrs; reconstruct collateral ligaments and tendons; preserve best-functioning digit
Office suture ligation within first 3 months for pedunculated postaxial polydactyly; simple and safe

second_line_drugs.notes: Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure for thumb (preaxial) polydactyly; combines two digits into one functional thumb
Defer to age 1+ for complex preaxial polydactyly to allow ossification for accurate anatomic assessment
Surgery at ~1 yr before walking; excise border toe preserving metatarsal alignment; minimize gait impact

notes: Surgical timing should be individualized based on polydactyly type, location, and complexity. Earlier surgery (before age 3) yields better postoperative finger shaping and functional recovery.
guideline_changes: Recent evidence favors surgery at 6 mo-3 yrs for better functional and cosmetic outcomes vs. age 4-6

first_line_drugs.notes: Fit when child pulls to stand (~9-12 mo); update annually during growth; multidisciplinary team approach
Gait training, strength, balance; adaptive equipment; ongoing as prosthetic needs change with growth
Limb lengthening (Ilizarov or IM nail) for partial defects; individualized based on defect severity

second_line_drugs.notes: Newer motorized IM nails (PRECICE) for older children; less external hardware; standard procedure now
Ankle disarticulation for severe fibular hemimelia; enables prosthetic fitting with end-bearing stump
For proximal femoral focal deficiency; rotates foot 180 degrees to serve as biological knee joint
AFO or KAFO for mild defects; shoe lifts for leg length discrepancy <2cm

notes: Decision between limb reconstruction and amputation with prosthetic fitting must involve family and multidisciplinary team. Prosthetic technology advances rapidly, requiring frequent reassessment.
source: AAPM&R Pediatric Limb Deficiency, JPOSNA 2024 Prosthetics Review
guideline_changes: 2024 advances in intramedullary nail lengthening and extramedullary implantable lengthening devices

first_line_drugs.notes: Fit at sitting age (~6 mo); maintains arm length symmetry; supports bimanual tasks and balance
Introduce at age 2-3 with prehensor; harness-operated; durable for active children
Bimanual training, adaptive techniques, fine motor skills; ongoing with prosthetic transitions

second_line_drugs.notes: Sensor-controlled powered hand; fit from age 2-3; multi-articulated digits available (i-Limb, Michelangelo)
Index finger transfer to thumb position for radial club hand or thumb aplasia; age 6-18 months
Distraction osteogenesis for partial defects (e.g., short forearm); case-by-case basis
Some children function well without prosthesis using adaptive techniques; reassess every 2-3 years

notes: Many children with unilateral upper limb deficiency adapt remarkably well with or without prostheses. Prosthetic choice should prioritize function and the child's own preferences as they develop.
source: AAPM&R Pediatric Limb Deficiency, JPOSNA Prosthetics Essentials
guideline_changes: Multi-articulated myoelectric prostheses (i-Limb) now available for children with gesture control software

first_line_drugs.notes: Release at 6-24 mo; dorsal flap commissuroplasty with full-thickness skin grafts; staged if adjacent webs
Prioritize thumb-index and ring-small web spaces first (by 6 mo) to prevent growth deviation

second_line_drugs.notes: Local flap designs avoiding skin graft donor site; suitable for simple incomplete syndactyly
Never release both sides of same digit simultaneously; 3+ months between stages to protect vascularity
Bony separation, tendon/nerve reconstruction for osseous syndactyly; higher complication rate
Toe syndactyly often does not require surgery unless functional impairment or shoe-fitting problems

notes: Release before age 2 prevents angular deformity in digits of unequal length. Staged surgery is mandatory when adjacent web spaces are involved to protect digit blood supply.
guideline_changes: Graftless syndactyly release techniques gaining adoption, reducing donor site morbidity

diagnosis_name: Other congenital malformation syndromes affecting multiple systems

first_line_drugs.notes: CMA, targeted gene panels, or exome sequencing; confirm specific syndrome; family recurrence counseling
Cardiac echo, renal US, ophthalmology, audiology, skeletal survey tailored to specific syndrome
Plot on syndrome-specific growth charts if available; GH evaluation for short stature syndromes (e.g., PWS)
Early intervention PT/OT/speech; behavioral support; educational planning and IEP from age 3

second_line_drugs.notes: Abdominal US q3mo until age 7 for Wilms tumor/hepatoblastoma; AFP levels q3mo until age 4
Somatropin 0.035 mg/kg/day; improves body composition, growth, cognition; exclude OSA before starting
Annual echo for aortic root dilation; beta-blocker (atenolol/losartan) if root Z-score >3
Strict caloric control and supervision for hyperphagia; behavioral strategies; dietitian involvement

notes: Q87 encompasses diverse syndromes requiring syndrome-specific management protocols. Accurate genetic diagnosis is the critical first step to guide appropriate surveillance and treatment.
source: ACMG 2023, BWS Consensus 2024, AAP PWS Health Supervision, Marfan/GeneReviews
guideline_changes: 2024 Italian BWS update expanded tumor surveillance; 2024 PWS guidance for transition to adult care published
